Ngoài những giá trị lịch sử mà có thể đọc,  biết được ở bất cứ phương tiện nào,  thì ở đây chỉ nhìn thấy đẹp, chứ chụp lên hình thì không đẹp,  vì bị chặn lại không cho đến gần.
Vài cảnh gọi là tạo dựng hiện trường,  nhưng nếu xét  lại vào thời điểm  năm 1975 thì nhìn rất sai.
Nếu so sánh với  Bến Nhà Rồng,  viện bảo tàng Sài Gòn,  hoặc các địa danh di tích khác,  thì nơi đây bán giá vé 40k  1 người là quá mắc.  Ngay cả  Lăng Bác,  hoặc một khu di tích lớn như  Kim Liên,  Làng Sen quê Bác  còn không bán vé,  mở cửa tự do cho nhân dân vào xem.

Kiến trúc của Dinh Độc Lập là dạng kiến trúc đối xứng, rất đẹp. Nhìn tổng thể, khuôn viên cây xanh rất nhiều, tạo cảm giác rất thoải mái. Mình vào đây vì một lần đi xem triển lãm kiến trúc nên không có tham quan nhiều ở không gian bên trong của dinh. Nhưng nhìn qua lớp kính tầng trên thì thấy rất đẹp. Và vì là nằm ngay trung tâm quận 1, nên ai cũng dễ dàng nhìn thấy nó nếu đi ngang nhà thờ Đức Bà. Đây là một địa điểm nên ghé qua một lần nếu đến Sài Gòn. Giá vé vào cửa là 30 nghìn/ người lớn.

Lần đầu vô. Rất sạch sẽ, đẹp và đầy đủ chứng tích lịch sử, còn khá mới vì lúc xây lại cũng gần đây và được trùng tu thường xuyên. Đông vì mình đi vào cuối tuần. Có thẻ học sinh, sinh viên là được giảm giá vé vào. Cũng giống như bảo tàng thôi không có gì thú vị đâu :)))))

Conned us out of entrance fee. Paid full price for all inclusive, given a sticker. Not allowed onto electric ride. Paid top price, not given ticket, only a sticker, not allowed onto electric car, went back to kiosk, they did not remember us, security guard who gave us sticker did not remember us, we are quite distinct in appearance, kiosk attendant accused us of picking sticker up ‘in the field’. Tried to rip us off so we would pay again-we paid cash-they could not find transaction! ! Ensure you have a receipt, a ticket, pay by card. Terrible customer service. Wouldn’t mind but we interacted with two people ….. neither could remember us.! It was literally about 30 minutes between paying and them not knowing us. It was not that busy! I had even returned to kiosk to check where electric car could be boarded. Tourists beware.

Boring and zero character palace. Tasteless , boring and zero character. That's how I would sum up this palace. Rooms with some chairs and telephones. A complete waste of time. Exhibition gallery was little interesting and worth paying a visit

Waste of time! Do not go there Don’t go there unless you are passionate about the palace. Go to the War Remnants museum instead, we sadly got the two mixed up and ended up in there. When you go through the ticket office, the member of staff wasn’t helpful at all. When you go to rent an audio guide, they want your hotel key card as deposit, strange. With the current heat, it was painful getting around with lifts that do not work, no AC and an extremely rare fan. The tours groups take the mick, they try and pressure you into giving up your spot so they can take their million photos.

BE CAREFUL WHEN VISITING INDEPENDENT PALACE. SECURITY STAF MAKES MONEY WHEN VISITORS BRING CAMERA. Today, July 3, I have an appointment with my friend to visit the Independence Palace and take pictures. After seeing on Facebook many people shared very impressive photos, I also wanted to take photos at places associated with this city's history, partly to combine sightseeing because I have never been here before. I would like to confirm in advance that I am just a photography enthusiast, not working or doing any business with photography. I often go to non-profit concept photography , take photos of my friends, never making money from photography or working for any photography studio. I know many monuments, museums don't allow taking pictures with cameras, so I carefully asked the people who took photos there before and they confirmed that if I go with small group 2-3 people without having to drag the crew with FLASH lights or extras, dont taking commercial photos with costume accessories, dont taking wedding photos, so you dont need to buy a photography package priced at 1,100,000vnd. Be more careful, I contact with the Fanpage of the Independence Palace (with a decent green stick) and the fanpage replied to me the same way. So excited to meet on Sunday afternoon to take pictures. When I got to the place where I bought the ticket and entered first, took out the camera to set up the parameters and took a few pictures of the landscape in advance, I went over and the security guard didn't say anything. When my friend came and called me, I went back to the gate area to give the ticket to my friend, then this happened. Security guard named Nguyen Xuan Thao saw that I was bringing a camera, then he stopped and said 1 sentence that I am a STUDIO STAFF taking professional photos and did not allow my friend to enter the pallace. I just asked if there is any proof that I am a professional Studio staff, security guard Thao said that it was because my friend did makeup and I was holding a camera, then it could only be a professional photography studio. What's wrong guys, normal people can't take camera and makeup? Only studio and studio crew can do that?. After arguing for a while, security guard Thao forced me to leave my backpack at the counter with the reason that the rule was NOT to bring luggage into the pallace. My backpack has 1 camera and 2 coconut cakes but the security guard insists that the backpack is huge and bulky, and this is Luggage, so it has to be left at the counter not to be brought in. At that time, many other guests brought backpacks that were even bigger than me (see the clip, you will see) but the security guard did not send them, only caught me. I agree to send it but refuse to send the camera, because the counter says it's valuables, money, important papers, valuables that customers keep themselves, the counter doesn't accept sending. Then the camera is also more than 10 million is already worth it, so I refused to send it and the security guard named Thao started all kinds of lies. This security guard asked me to buy 1 million tickets for the studio to take wedding photos with a crew of less than 5 people to let me in. I was really surprised and amazed at the obvious and uneducated way of making money by the security guards here. When I said I was not a studio and did not take wedding or commercial photography and asked this employee to prove it, security guard Nguyen Xuan Thao had a very uneducated and rude behavior - rushed to grab the phone out of my hand, trying to attack me. Very hooligan behavior like in the clip. After that, in the end, the argument didn't go anywhere, I was also fed up with the way the Independence Palace managed by giving full authority to the guard without a customer care department or management supervision so that the This is free to abuse power, make money for visitors like that. Even when I asked to see the customer service department to solve it, the security guard here didn't let me and sent another security guard to receive my feedback. They don't record the incident, they simply write it down on a scrap of paper. I know that my feedback will also go to the trash because it is also received by another security guard, it has no value, so I come here to present this incident for you to be wary when coming to the Palace. Independent visits with a camera, don't let these powerless security guards make money and show that disrespectful attitude.

DO NOT get on a cyclo here Myself and partner asked for a short ride from the palace to the war museum and we were taken to the wrong place. Threatened and asked for a lot more money than was agreed at the start. Do not trust the price they state at the start. My advice do not use!
What an absolute waste of time . A few rooms with some furniture, and a few more rooms with a desk and some telephones. There is nothing interesting at all
Not much to see - rooms with decadent decor. Gives a bit of history up to 1975 Independence. Go if have time to kill with cheap entrance fee.
Local history building, big, ceremonial,not really worth the time visiting. In the garden there are some local mature and tall trees
Boring concrete structure. If you don't have anything else to do then may be better off going to the Ben Thanh market or having a relaxing massage then seeing this soul-less concrete building .

You'll have to spend 175k dongs per person if you want an audio guide (without it, the visit is quite worthless, 40k for the entry plus 75k for the audio guide). Considering we are in Vietnam, this is seriously expensive.
Sadly on the day we visited the Palace was preventing entry between 1100 & 1300. Closed for lunch. As we were on an organised trip we were unable to return that day.

Watch out for these guys outside the perimeter of the palace. They take you on a cheap tour supposedly at 1500 dong per hr. By the end, they charge you 100x that amount (around $350 for 4 people) These scammers drop you off to places you don't know, with fewer tourists. Won't let you go until you pay; they literally took the cash out of our bags...police dont do anything. BE CAREFUL!!!
Palace is just big, not very nice. The palace they rebuilt was the ugliest design they could choose. The only OK part was the bunkers.

Just walked around, had a quick look, nothing particular to even take a photo. Entry ticket is $40.000VND. The elephant foot really puts me off, and there are even 3 different sizes, tour guide said there are real. I feel sorry for those animals, was it really necessay to have this as a decoration back that time?
Communist building, ugly, nothing to see. Does not worth the time and money to visit. The palace was rebuilded, looks very much like a communist factory, not a palace.

Iconic but unimpressive Minimalist in design and content. There were information boards in English but the overall experience was quite haphazard, a lack of direction as to which route to take meant we spent a lot of time walking around the same area. There was no air con inside which made it hot and stuffy especially in the bunker room. When we visited there was a marquee up in front of the palace which restricted the view down the main route in front of the Palace and obscured the iconic gates. Overall, symbolic significance is greater than its content.

Nothing of any interest in here. A few government meeting rooms on one floor, then the bunker has a bunch of small rooms with old desks and telephones. Seriously, dont waste your time, go do anything else.

Wasn’t really interested in this. Paid and attended, the food stall there is crap. There so rude and they threw the food on the table in front of us. The two tanks at the front aren’t what they’re said to be; hate it when people make up random crap about defence vehicles. The place smelt pretty gross, it’s really not worth paying to physically go in. Others may have loved it, but personally thought it was crap...
Very boring place, shouldn't go there To be honest, very boring to me, so I have nothing to say about it. I will tell friends to avoid it.

The tour guide was extremely helpful however the tour itself of the palace went for very long and it was very very hot and packed. Did go however on a weekend so it may be best to go mid-week. Went in a group and it was difficult for everyone to remain together which meant some people missed out on seeing and hearing certain things. Sights inside the building were quite boring. Lifts were not working. Would not go again if I were to return to Ho Chi Minh.
Avoidable Soviet Style building Nothing too special about it.Was part of half day city tour so we had to visit. You won't miss a lot by skipping it

2nd visit. First time was 2012 when I didn't know the history behind it. Now I understand and comprehend why the palace is quite basic and not as impressive or showy as one may expect a palace to be. My advice: read up on the history Behind the palace and assess if you still want to go. a few things are replicas and not the real thing. I recommend spending your time visiting other places of interest instead Even though entry is cheap. Several parts weren't accessible. Gardens are beautiful. Best Feature of the palace.
If you are in favor of visiting '70 style offices and meeting rooms, you'll probably will enjoy it the fullest. The other 99% of the world will find this place not worth the visit

A good spacious palace It is situated at the Centre of the city. Easy to take taxi. Audio tour is available if you wish to know more about the place. They are well maintained and clean. Elevators available to anyone who wants to take.

I found the Independence palace a waste of time. Looking at some carpet, chairs and some 1960 telecoms equipment was not that interesting. Knowing that a tank was driven up on Independence Day to under the balcony was the peak of interest.

Nothing much here really. A bit formal areas. Some people got very claustrophobic in the tunnels below and there were so many people you ended up standing there. Only took dong fir drinks on the rooftop .

One time is one time to mucho This monument of history is not worth to call it a muesum. Well the bunker is very interesting BUT you get nothing to know about Vietnam War (or as it's called here America War). They only Show the rooms with the old furniture and give Information like "This is the dining hall, people went here for diner". The bunker is the only interesting thing about the Independence Palace!

It's just like walking in park. It might be better to have a tour guide. The building inside are quite empty, I guess they did preserve the item fully. And it's pretty hot because of the building construct, bring water.

Make sure you leave yourself plenty of time before closing time I googled the opening times to make sure we could plan our day accordingly and the first result that showed was 9 am - 5 pm. This is incorrect. The actual opening times are 7.30 am - 11.30 am and then opens again at 1.00 pm - 5.pm. On the booklet that they give you at the ticket office it specifies that the morning opening times are 7.30 am - 12.00 pm. We arrived at 10.45 and we bought our tickets but they didn't warn us that we would need to literally run around in order to see everything and read the information. It consists of 4 floors and a basement. As we'd reached the first floor, the security guards started locking the doors and restricted access to parts of the tour and crossed their arms at me in true Vietnamese fashion saying, 'closed'! gesturing toward the door to move along. As we were ushered impatiently through the rest of the tour up the floors, I hurriedly read the small amount of information provided on the rooms (Security guard on my heels). Luckily I've read a lot about Vietnam's history as there is no context provided on the very specific events mentioned on the plaques. When we were approaching the exit gates and were around 5 metres away, the security guard decided to lock them and walk away, forcing us to walk the entire length of the court to the far gate in order to leave. Do they want us to leave or stay!? I'm confused. What's infuriating is that as soon as you've cleared the area, the staff just sit down and start playing on their phone. It seems that all of this is done so the entire staff of the imperial palace can go on lunch. Why do they close an entire government building/ museum and go for lunch all at the same time?? On top of this, visiting this place didn't give any more insight into Vietnam or its history. I got more of an insight eating the street food.

Firstly I found it really hard to find the entrance and was yelled rudely by security guards for going to the wrong place. The Palace is so sterile inside, to me it just felt like an exhibition of conference rooms with a little information on the history. I don't think it's worth the entrance free unless you're particularly interested and this means something to you, I wouldn't recommend visiting just for fun.

This "used" to be the American embassy, it was later used as the Russian consulate. It is now a gig building with some really old furniture such as "boardroom" tables...that's it !!!! unless you know the "real" history of this place don't waste your time or money....

Big empty rooms, just like most houses in Vietnam. You can see some elephant feet that have been turned into foot stools. The guide has very little information to give, the internet offers more. And any political questions will go unanswered.

I was expecting something unique to show me , but when I get inside full of tourist lots of people around 1pm , and the place just an old rooms nothing special to be honest , only cool thing a helicopter on the top floor but you can see more helicopter in the war museum. View also disappointed really if you skip this place probably you make a right decision. There are much better places and museum you can spend more time .
Uninspired architecture with very little story to tell. No worthwhile works if art, furnishings or embellishments. Captures the cultural depth of the era!!

Perhaps we didn't enjoy this very much because of our age but we found reunification palace to be a bit dull. The architecture was interesting and the gardens were beautiful but overall it just wasn't for us. While the rooms were pretty there wasn't really much to see and we were too hot and bothered to appreciate the history as we should have.

I visited this place because I found nothing much to do in Saigon. I found this city one of the most boring cities I have ever visited. If you are looking for luxury or extravagance, then this palace is not for you. This place is very small and simple. An English speaking guide is available on request but I am not interested in any history so I didn't need the guide. There is no aircon (just like all other places in Saigon I ever visited) so it's really hot. Some halls and rooms are empty and the rest don't have much to display. In my opinion, this place is such a waste of time especially if you're not interested in Vietnamese history. I didn't take any pictures because nothing's worth a shot.

Of course this is memorable for the iconic regime change moment the tanks rolled in but (for us) unless you are a big history geek, the 60s decor and various rooms where x met y aren't worth time if your visit is brief..

This place is a waste of time and quite boring. Don't bother coming here, nothing to see apart from a few corridors & old furnitures & bunker was not impressive. While the exterior of the building is quite nice and it's interesting how the architect designed the building, and that this was the site of the end of the Vietnam War when a North Vietnamese Army tank crashed through its gates - we wouldn't recommend going here.
Unless you study some sort of how to make rooms and buildings very ugly - it really is boring there. Sorry :/
Worst visit ever no audio guide no information 10 room with very breaf description. Not even nive looking. No history about vietnam. Maybe if you hire a private guide.
The Independence Palace is a 'moth balled' building that in my opinion would only be attractive to students of history. Give it a miss.
Slightly boring. quick talk from the outside about the history etc would have been sufficient for us as we found the trapesing around rooms tedious
It is a total waste of time. They show you some rooms with a bunch of tables and chairs that you can't even touch. Thrust me don't go. It is also quite expensive for vietnam (like 30.000 dongs).

Carpets, chairs and more carpets Carpets, chairs and more carpets!! That's actually all there is! So if you like carpets and chairs and phones on the desks, then this place is for you. When we saw the sign bunker we got kind of lifted up but then empty rooms with tables, phones and chairs again. The most boring museum I've ever been to;(

Closed on the Day of Our Visit Apparently it is not unusual for the palace to close on any given day with no notice to the public. That's what happened on the day of our visit, thus leading our tour guide to apologize and admit his embarrassment at being surprised by the closure.

Warning: Became victim of virtual daylight mugging. Near the site entrance an official looking tour guide suggested rickshaw ride to a couple of nearby sites for vnd 15,000. He assigned a waiting rickshaw. After first site rickshaw puller took me to a desolate zone and demanded 1.5 million vnd. Not taking any chances at unknown location gave whatever I had in pocket. These scamsters are apparently operating with full knowledge of authorities right in front of the palace.

tired and boring place Again this is a tired and boring place . There is no free tour guide. They are closed for lunch for 2 hours every day with the exception of the souvenir shop which sold overpriced souvenir . Stay off this place unless you are a history buff

Spent 25mins here and that was more than enough. Not much to see. Imposing from the outside and that's pretty much it. Spent more time sitting on the swing in the back garden. Paid $30k for an adult and $5k for child tickets.
Historical importance - 10/10 Worth seeing from the outside - definitely Worth going inside - I wouldn't bother. Very hot, lots of same same rooms...

This was a complete waste of time. The palace is by far a beautiful palace. It's just an empty shell with nothing to see but a few old telephones and empty filing cabinets. There is little information displayed to describe the history of it so you learn little.
I found this place not very interesting as the palace wasn't in great condition. There also isn't any air condition which made it very uncomfortable.

Don't wasted your time here if you come without a tour guide. There has many ex-used room to shows a period furniture and a small information stand in front of the room. I spent my time here to find out the correct way to walk and understand the story. So complex not clear I have to complain this place is a worst museum or exhibition which i have seen.

If you have time to waste. Depressing There is nothing exciting in this building. Rooms are left over with no explanation and are poorly managed, the overall feeling is rather depressing. Only the basement is worth a 5 minutes check. Don't lose your time in visiting this honestly. Go on the other side of the park you will see beautiful remaining colonial houses instead.

Really rude staff both selling tickets and checking tickets, Incredible that they have jobs. Inside the grounds are okay and the old tanks worth a picture or two. Inside I'm afraid I found it extremely dull and couldn't wait to escape - ok if you want to look at some old style furniture and old radios and phones. I did not enjoy this unfortunately. The War remnants museum on the other hand was excellent.
Not worth a visit. Can't see many people saying it was good. And if I have to put in one hundred characters I will tell u again it was not worth wasting time.
Nothing to see! Not even the park It wasn't interesting for us. Not even the park around the palace worth paying the entrance fee. Better spend your time somewhere else.
Not worth the visit. yes it has tanks and a helicopter but generally it sucks. weird tourist attraction!

I must just say right off that I am reviewing this for the exterior ONLY. (We could not have access as they were setting up for the 40 year celebration at end April.) And as I said, an ugly building and not worth getting out the bus for... However, it may well be wonderful inside. If it is, I'm sorry to have missed it.

There is so little to see here that you should spend your 30.000 vnd on sipping 2 cans of Coca-Cola over the span of one hour. No A/C to spare you from the heat, either. The neatest thing was the wall sconces in the wife's entertaining space which look a bit like a chrysanthemum. Skip this one.

We were really disappointed. There is literally nothing here. Just a building with some large rooms with nondescript furniture. Terrible waste of time. I have seen Government Offices that are far better and that's not saying much.

Save time and do something else After persuasive tour guide won the dispute of "where it is worth going in Saigon" the decision was taken to visit " Palace of Reunification". It is the most unimpressive, dull building I have seen in a long time. It was just a course of 60's socialism, with boring and dated meeting rooms, not dated enough to surprise and raise interest neither fascination. My local school back home is more fascinating... The supermarket can easily raise more interest ... I have seen petrol station rather more intriguing... Please do yourself a favor and go for a walk on the streets of Saigon, a colorful and vibrant city, with a colonial feel and full of life ... don't waste your very expensive hours of your holiday in this totally unattractive place where no lessons for mind and spirit are to be found ..I am sorry that my home education did not allow me to just say after 10 minutes - I had enough! - I have waisted an hour politely listening to the most uninteresting political stories from a recent socialist era in a building that says nothing.

Watch out for coconut seller scam. They will trick u to carry the stick holding the coconuts & then open the coconuts to sell you at an exorbitant price. Coconuts selling in market for 20,000 VD but they demand 150,000VD. Stop them from opening coconuts. Offer tip of 20,000VD for picture if you feel generous

We hates it we hates it! OMG soooooooo boring, and the architecture is ATROCIOUS! It was like a bad 70s movie that just went on and on and burnt my retinas. For the love of all that is holy, I wanted to make it stop. Do yourself a favour and give it a miss. You can learn about the history of Vietnam without subjecting yourself to unimaginative lines, burnt orange carpets, and 80's furniture.

There really is nothing here to see. A few banquet rooms, meeting rooms and in the bunker old communications devices with one table and a chair. You would see this place only to realize where you stand (at the front gate) 40 years ago tanks burst through when they captured south Vietnam. Do not get guided tour. Waste of money.
You can wander around the place and see the function rooms and bedroom of the former president, vs the bunkers in the basement. Not really worth much. It's just a big area.
not worth wasting time at It comprises a series of rooms with ornately decorated tables. About the onyl cool thing was the army helicopter replica
No info on wearing casual for entry Disappointed as many tourists was locked out of the gates as they dont allow people wearing shorts entry. As weather was relatively hot many tourist wear short.
Unlike the Post Office, this building is just barely handsome. It is nothing special, and I do not recommend that you use your valuable time in Saigon visiting this building.

I've seen nicer hotels It's not a nice building to look at from the outside and the inside was no better. Some of the rooms reminded me of $10 hotel rooms. So boring, I wouldn't recommend it. I even did the free guided tour and the most interesting information was "this room is yellow so that it feels relaxing" zzzzzzzzzzzz

oh dear another state attraction.. well worth missing loads of meeting rooms and floors of naf gov props. really you are better going to a market or a park.. or staying in an air conditioned hotel room.
Don't waste your time! This place is like a glorified public high school in the states. The best thing was taking a picture in front of the tank in front of the palace.
Not a fan of this place at all. This is an old palace with just furniture everywhere. Not incredibly exciting and I'd say miss it and I wish I had my time back.
not sure what the attraction is. BORING. Many better places to take a stroll

This building was cheap to enter and easy to find but very boring the rooms have a few chairs and tables in but many rooms to look at and many floors with the same. The basement was the most interesting bit with all the old maps and telecommunications machines in. also the roof where you can get a drink and take a picture of the outside view.

I have been living in Saigon for 6 months now, and had avoided going here as it's not something I am interested in. However, when a friend came over from Australia, I said I'd do a half day tour with her. The Reunification Palace was the first of 3 places we went to. Basically it is a terribly out-dated, battered shell of what it likely was back when it was actually lived in. The rooms are sparsely furnished, the carpet is tatty, and there is honestly nothing really to see. We had a tour guide which at least explained rooms to us - if we had not have had him, we would have walked in and then straight out. Nothing historical or exciting about it, I'm sorry to say. Recommend bypassing this one!
boring office building has a bust of chairman Ho; the rest looks like any office building anywhere

The palace is impressive and of significancy in South Vietnamese history. But today it is only a propaganda spot for North Vietnamese communists. The visitors are being lied about the history and made believing that South Vietnam never existed as an independent country. When you listen to those guides you feel like in Moscow 1980 or in North Korea. They try to brainwash the visitors.

"Palace" is a gross overstatement While the grounds are beautiful, this modern concrete excuse for a palace was a total let down. It has been left the way it was when surrendered to the north, including putrid 70's style wall to wall carpets. Go to the central post office instead, much more impressive, and free!
The building looks gd from outside but there is nothing really much inside. Just showcasing some of the rooms that the president use. Ok to skip this.

Paid the general admission for both the palace and museum but seriously don't bother with the museum it's full of uninteresting propaganda. The palace itself is a cool building with a lot of history and opulent decor and furniture. The bunkers were a bit ordinary, some rooms had nothing but a desk and telephone or stacked radios on top of each other. The layout is very confusing, I couldn't find rooms on the map and stumbled across others without meaning to. DO NOT GET THE AUDIO GUIDE - it costs 90k (which is highway robbery to begin with) and they insist on you leaving ID or hotel key which I wasn't comfortable doing and the system stopped working half way through and they refused to refund me.

Call Tripadvisor is one of the top 10 things to see when we’re in Ho Chi Minh City. The entry fee was relatively inexpensive within had to go to the actual independence entry to receive our headphones for the actual tour. This was fine however, we found it very disappointing as most of the displays were very boring and washed down. The artifacts such as the library, presidential suite,and bomb shelter were very stripped down and poorly displayed. Didn’t really get anything out of it historically….more so a propaganda piece.

Go and visit it , but just it !! You must see it, good to remember old comunist style, Is not good becouse is a little bit not organised Such a nice outside without a very nice place for resting, just a cheap coffe shop

For us, it was part of the program. The place offers omly secondary exhibits like desks and dining table, quite likely from the late Soth Vitanamese presidentail family. The building itself is not interesting. In all, a might be visit, if you are close. But not worth heading there.

Beautiful building but visit without much interest, we are shown the outdated rooms of their ceremonies, many other closed rooms, not much to see, fortunately the visit costs only 40000 dong Automatically translated

Very little interest in this place, apart from the history of the architecture and the fact that it is inspired by Far Eastern character words meaning "pride" or "freedom". The tour is around the office and living rooms of the former president and vice president. Probably a little interest in 60s and 70s furniture Far from being a must-have, contrary to what is often said.
Unless you want to see 70’s style furniture then do not bother. The only good thing is the view of the street opposite for a photo.

We did this as part of a day tour, honestly I think is is just something to fill the day, nothing really see, just a bunch of dusty rooms. I don't want to be harsh but if you are on your own don't waste your time.

I've seen this twice, once in the morning in daylight and once at night with night lights on. It's slightly prettier at night, but it's not a very interesting building and you can't really get close to it, worth a walk by but it's only a 2 minute thing
Paid for extra attractions that didn't exist, you can enter palace by just paying general admission fee,not a lot too see

Can skippable if crunched of time. It is just an elaborate office with antique furniture. Nothing great unless you are fan of the Vietnam history Ns need to understand the details of which historical meeting took place when and where etc.

nothing to write home about This part of our private tour of Ho Chi Minh exploration could have been easily missed. Architecturally bland with questionable cleanliness of carpets and public spaces, it has nothing to add to the character of this vibrant and beautiful city.

Not worth the admission as there were only conference and meeting rooms on the first floor while other floors were not accessible. The Norodom Palace (actually it's a big old mansion) and had displays of the history of Vietnam.

I am completely disappointed about this visit. Nothing really to see... Totally enough to see the building from outside becuse inside you can see only 2 rooms (cabinets) and basement with kitchen. Besides we bought not just enterance tickets but also exhibition tickets, but we did not see any additional exhibition what so ever, money paid for nothing :D
Nothing much inside the building. Just some rooms of the past. No air con there too. Also, the counter gave us 2 different tickets which I don't know what the other pair of ticket is for. WEIRD.
Worth a visit if you are in the city. However, I found it quite underwhelming. A lot of nice furniture, art work, etc. is no longer at the palace. Very minimalistic.

I don’t like to be rude about the artefacts belonging to other countries and cultures, however TripAdvisor is about guiding visitors. The “palace” is a tribute to architecture and decor relating to the 1960s and 1970s. It does have a significant place in the history of the country, however, that’s it. The building is a concrete monster, with little warmth, or welcome. The state rooms, apartments, libraries and banqueting rooms are set in a dusty moment 40 years ago. The grounds are well maintained, but plain. If you like concrete corridors, 1970s furniture, and the remains of a war command centre, you may like this. I couldn’t wait to leave.
This visit was probably the worst place we have visited during our tour.a total waste of time very boring.

Could've been maintained better Part of most packaged city tours. Was disappointed to see the way temporary structures where been erected on the front lawn with scant regards to the historic values. Using grounds of such important locations for any kind of cultural activity gives the wrong impression.

They charge for tickets to get through the gates, once inside you can walk around on your own or purchase a tour. You can get an audio tour or a guided tour. The guided tour was really expensive, the audio tour is cheaper but definitely pretty pricey, you also need to surrender either a government ID or your hotel room key to rent the audio device. Walking around was really hot, there doesn't seem to be any AC or fans going, except for in the gift shop and top floor where you can purchase drinks. Definitely interesting to see some of the rooms, but honestly most of it was boring to look at and some rooms especially in the bunker all looked the same to me. If you are headed here, I'd skip the purchasing of a tour and just walk around and read the captions in each room.
The old French building I saw at the photos was really great. The new 60-70s style is not my style but anyway except basement war rooms that may have some interest rest of place is nothing really ,
My opinion is don't go, the people working here are rude and unfriendly and actually a little bit scary.

Afternoon tours in Ho Chi Minh City are tiring due to the heat of the atmosphere and some destinations that do not suit all the family, or different cultures .. This place was ours and even most of the tour people (different nationalities) was bored and tired uselessly ?! The palace is far from the gate, and there are only meeting rooms that do not belong to my culture. The atmosphere was warm and humid, the water machine and ice cream were only at the main gate !!

Closed between 11-1 but they still sell you tickets We entered the gardens after 11 and bought the tickets to see the Palace but to our surprise the place closed from 11 to 1 and we couldn’t visit it. Funny enough they sold the ticket. We didn’t go back

The exhibit on French involvement was very good and informative and had so great chats with Vietnamese people but this is extra. The Palace is in nice grounds but is very modern and lacking much history or uniqueness it could be an office block hence the only photo I have!

There were only a few palace rooms open so disappointed. The museum next to the palace offered more information so most of the time was spent inside there. But always worth a visit for tourists - can't remember the cost of admission.
Hi.. I personally didn’t see any good there... because it’s look like hotel to me with some old sofas and bed.. totally waste of money..
Maybe because it's a palace we were expecting it to be quite grand but it's not. Very 60,s feel. Obviously a li g went on here during the time of the war and you can find out a lot.

I really didn’t like the independence palace. There isn’t much to see really and the documentary room in English wasn’t working. I looked up how the palace looked during the French colony, such a shame they completely changed how it looked like.

Must say I did no research but have been to other ‘palaces’ before so had some expectations. This building I saw today did not resemble one. If you didn’t know it was a palace, you would think it was a conference centre with lots of rooms displaying different types of seminar or meeting setups available for clients to choose from. Didn’t really get anything out of my walk around but the heat has been so relentless the palace offered an hour’s worth of shade, so no complaints there.

The previously name Presidential Palace, always good, except..... Taking a first time visitor to the Presidential Palace but had forgotten the staff lunch hour (and a half). Yes, don't pay the entry fee after about 0930 as the staff unceremoniously boot you out as their unmissable lunch break arrives, from about 1130 the ushering out starts. Then don't try a 4pm entry either as crossed arms at the gate means go away, come back another day? Shoulders shrug do they care, not that I ever saw. This attraction is a fascinating one and steeped in history, all so well preserved it could still be 1974. Spoilt by the over officious staff when knock-off time nears. Early am or early pm or not recommended.

Completely useless building This was a waste of time. Only furniture on display. And old rotary style phones on tables. Just looks fake. Minimal information displayed. The basement bunker with telecommunications stuff may be interesting to techies but no significant information there either. Highly avoidable. Just look up on Wikipedia and YouTube.

Little bit disappointed, even though it’s call ‘Palace’, don’t expect you will see a glorious type of European or Japanese Palace. If you have time and nothing to do and want to spend about 1 or 2 hours before another meal, just spend VND$68000 to learn some history.
There are so many other plases to goe, soe if you doen’t like this kind of arcitecture then goe somewere else
Just a big boring ugly building with not much of interest to see inside. Find something else to do instead of this. It’s hot and stuffy inside too. Don’t bother.
Not a must visit place If you are interested in seeing meeting rooms, furnitures, carpets etc only go for it. Will not recomment tbis as a must visit as I couldnt find it interesting.
Great for History buffs The Palace didn’t really grab my attention, it felt a little sterile. Perhaps the most interesting part was the kitchen area and the “mod cons” they had back in the day.

The only thing fascinating about the place are two tanks which historically were the first ones to enter the building to overthrow the southern VIetnamese government and unify Vietnam . The indoors didn’t interest me at all. Typical administrative building . For those who have visited a lot of them, this one is not so interesting .

If you like boring architecture, frozen in time, then go for it! Actually, if you are doing the tourist circuit, you have to check it out, just because it's cheap. Word on the street is the audio tour is necessary. I generally don't like audio tours, so passed. The highlight for me is the basement bunkers and various maps there. That's a highlight, for sure.
Not bad, but not that great either and whilst only 40k dong, compared to the excellent War Remnants Museum that seemed poor value. OK to fill an hour, but nothing special.
A rather dull and uninteresting place. There are many better ways to spend your time in Ho Chi Minh City
Relatively modern building used by the south Vietnam nation then Rooms showing meeting rooms , bunkers etc Souvenir shops as usual
Teenagers were very bored here was not a huge amount to look at. I would recommend families skip this one as it’s quite boring. Lots of tourists and repetitive views
Install Air conditioning!!! This could have been interesting but visit was ruined by lack of air conditioning or fans Impossible to enjoy during the summer without

Visited here because of its historical significance. For that reason it's worth having a look at but inside the palace is mostly presidential rooms (well preserved and like stepping back in time) but quickly lost interest. It's very cheap to get into so a quick wander around does no harm.

Go if you like history The place is nice if you're interested in history. Otherwise, it can be a really unpleasant experience, we went on a normal day and it was super hot, as there are no air conditioners or fans anywhere in the building.
Took 5 min to look around.. took pic of meeting rooms and ... that was it. If you do decide to check the place out I recommend getting tour guide

Just another building. This is just another building with only one specific room having historical significance Apart from That there is nothing great to see. Recommend only an outside view that’s all and some pics.

A former palace that's not so special as one would think Marked as the site of independence and the end to civil war, the site is just that, a site for the end. There is zero historic information on the day of independence or the moments leading up to it, the palace itself simply showcases how it was once used by the South Vietnamese government, with some simple placs on what each room was used for during the government reign. It's not interesting if you turn up wanting to know about the historic place of independence and what occurred. The placs are repetitive and the information very basic, you are literally just visiting a traditional palace which apparently continues to be used today, although this is unclear. I don't quite understand the rating, I feel it's become a tourist gimic as a famed site and a photo opportunity, but it's not worth visiting unless you have nothing to do.

For History lover only This place suits only for people who want to explore about remains of Republic of Vietnam (South Vietnam). Nothing special inside the palace. This place only displays old furnitures and rooms which tell us about all events occured in it.
The palace has no interesting history. It is a modern imitation of old-time europian palaces, a bit pathetic. Not clear why they need it, apart from glorifying the present regime.
Not my thing at all. If you are into office block 60s architecture or the whole Vietnam post colonial stuff then it's for you. Frankly, if you give this a 5 you need to get out more.

Don't take the kids!! Not my thing but maybe if you're really into history this might be more interesting. The place is basically rooms and rooms containing different sets of tables and chairs. That's it. Tables and chairs. There were plenty of kids there and every single one of them looked bored senseless. I really wouldn't recommend as part of a family holiday unless you want your kids to hate you! The only interesting park is the bunker and even that didn't float my boat.
I personally found this a waste of time. I gave it two stars because maybe it was just me. I just thought it was a pointless stop off and tour when the city offers so much more

The first day we arrived in Ho Chi Minh City, we drove past a 3 story commercial building surrounded by fences in a park like setting. My first thought was that it was an embassy. Two days later went on a "City Tour". Was I ever surprised to drive up to the same commercial building and learn it was a "palace". I guess when you're the government, you can call it whatever you want. I call it an office building without the offices. The building is furnished with a bunch of late 60's and early 70's things. It's kind of like stepping back in time. No flat screens; no computers but nothing that you wouldn't see from watching "That 70's Show". Skip this one unless you're a real history buff.

1960s Architecture On A Grand Scale This palace replaced a previous French building that was bombed in 1960s. It's large, reminds me of an office block and whilst it features various large entertaining rooms with ostentatious furnishings it really didn't do it for us. The trouble is that it doesn't really stand comparison to stately homes or palaces, of a different age, that are available to view elsewhere. In many respects the most interesting feature is the story of the storming of the gates by the VC in the war.

It's a big government building with very uninspiring architecture inside and out. Some of the art in the rooms and the maps in the bunker were interesting bit other than that not brilliant. Worth it for a stroll around but don't expect much.

They close in the middle of the day We went there just before they closed for a couple of hours during the day. We were not informed when we entered the area so when we were in, after a little while exploring we were told to leave, we did not even get to see the bunker. They then closed the gate and the ticket office so we had to walk around to find the exit. They do not even give you a return ticket so you can come back to finish. There also should be a map of the area for efficiency.

Very Plain and Dull for a Palace Quite surprised at how plain this Palace is, quite possibly one the most plain and dull I have ever been to. Compared to the many in Europe, it is really uninteresting. The actual building is very ordinary to look at and the rooms inside are not much more than some chairs, tables and a carpet, with basic descriptions of what the rooms were used for and by whom. The bunker is at least a little more interesting having old communications materials and equipment and various basic rooms of the President are other staff. It is worth a quick stroll through just to see and move on. The grounds of the Palace are very nice to walk around, and that's as good as it gets. Can be easily seen in an hour, even with kids.

If you're a history buff or someone who's interested in political affairs (really more on where the central government have meetings, talks, etc.), go here. Otherwise, this place can be skipped. The palace is spacious and amazing, but I truly felt I was just visiting a place where they're showing off some furniture and rooms.
Nothing much to do or see at here.It just showcase meeting room and resident for government servant.Personally I feel that boring.You will need 1 hours to explore the building.

Maybe I feel that way because we spend 1.5 hours there, but come on its just the place that the president used to live. It does have a historic meaning, but overall it was an exceptionally boring visit.

My girlfriend and I visited the Parliament early in the morning(opens from 7.30am) and so had a chance to walk around without many other tourists present. There are many rooms to view as well as the war bunker but you don't have much opportunity to take decent photos as you can't actually enter very many of them. Also while having a few insights into the war, there realistically is very little going on and it really isn't on par with many parliament buildings in other parts of he world. Tickets are cheap at €2 each to enter, but really nothing to write home about.

If it had not been for the very numerous stairs and grand corridors, I would have learned everything I needed to know in less than 15 minutes flat. The bunker area is interesting. The grand rooms which were used for official functions lack any atmosphere of feeling the importance of these rooms. The sparse information provided sufficient details to understand context, process of transfer of power after truce, etc. There are so many other museums, eg. Remnants museum (a must to visit). The grounds an the palace are certainly spectacular in maintenance and architecture. I would however be happy just to walk around the palace, as from other museums one learned much more about the strategic command centres in the fields.
Go only if you have nothing much to do It is just a building of history. While it contains some good documentations of the war it can be skipped.
Yep it's an OK building, but that's about it. Do your homework or you will struggle to work out where this palace fits with Vietnamese history

kind of disappointing. not much explanation. not much things said and history. there is not much to understand if u dont have a tour guide. the place is kind of dry. they could do more to let tourists understand the importance and the political bkgrd.
Just a building built in the 1960s with some dated furniture. Nothing much to read inside of interest.
Somewhat underwhelming I went there with my two teens. To be honest it just looked like an old government building. The bunker was the most interesting part.

My family and I found this palace is very boring.It was just a display of the bedrooms and meeting rooms. Nothing impressive except the history behind it. The entire place is not air conditioned, I wouldn't recommend

My family and I went on a city tour, and the first stop was Independence Palace. The tour guide led us through the entire palace, stopping at EVERY room to basically reiterate what was on the plaque. The entire group felt the tour through the Palace repetitive, and it took over an hour to complete. The pace could've been quickened, and it would've been cooler to learn more about the bunkers. All in all, don't go with a tour to the Palace; instead, travel at your own pace.

We had been told that this was one of the major attractions in Ho Chi Minh City, and whilst it looks brilliant from the outside, there is practically nothing to see inside, just a few rooms with very little information about them. If you are expecting to learn about the palace, then go with a tour guide, because otherwise it just looks like a large mansion with ornate furniture, not very interesting with very little to see. It took us 10 minutes to go around the whole palace. Would not recommend going inside without a tour guide, not sure if it would be that good even with one.

First of all don't do what I did and arrive just after 11am. Seems Saigon attractions have a siesta time so I had to kill a few hours with none of things I planned on seeing open. Made the mistake of walking a lot so by the time I could get in, my feet were sore and I was sweaty and gross. Perhaps these things coloured my view but I was in and out of here quickly. It's an ugly building which is unsurprising given when it was built and there's really nothing to engage you. Here's a big room with a conference table & 70s brutalist decor, here's another room with a work table and even more brutal 70s decor. The tank out the front which is allegedly the one that broke down the gates (by far the most attractive part of the entire building and surrounds FWIW) is the most interesting part. Maybe worth a quick trip early in the day before getting on to the more interesting parts of Saigon.

it's not great place for visit there are bizzar furniture in this place you can find any amazing think in this meuseum but its not incredible . theese are very nirmal things . i think if you are interesting culture and history ; you should go to street and looking around the city . you recieve to better thing

I have very mixed feelings after visiting this place. The mention of a Palace initially brought about a pre-conceived sense of grandeur and magnificence. Unfortunately for a building that once served as a Presidential Palace, the experience was underwhelming, to say the least. I wouldn't go so far as to call it disappointing, but I would say that if the relatively simplistic interior decorations are a true reflection of how the Vietnamese presidents used to reside, then I truly admire their humility. The exterior, true to communist style architecture, was not ornate, but instead, an attempt to reflect an imposing, monolithic structure to exude a sense of authority. It is hard not to compare this palace with the lavish furnishings of many other Presidential palaces in the world, although some may say it is unfair to do so. On one hand, I appreciate having the opportunity to visit a palace of a unique style and culture. On the other hand, I have to say honestly that I was not the least impressed by this palace.
A fleeting tourist attraction! This attraction is gated. It takes a few minutes to see and take pictures. It is within walking distance of the War Remnants Museum.

This is not a palace in the sense of what you might picture in Europe etc. Although the architecture from the outside is quite interesting to look at. Actually inside, there is a number of rooms on display but most of them are quite plain and functional so it doesnt take long to do the tour and see what is there. There is no air conditioning inside so it is stiflingly hot. The documentary at the end was informative and worth a watch.

A bit underwhelming but I probably was expecting too much. You see the variety of rooms used for planning and entertaining. Down the basement, you see a variety of old and mechanical communication And administrative tools. not for me personally but it may interest others.
While it played a major roll in the history of the Vietnam war, to the uneducated it is just another old building.

President house of Vietnam We went with tour guy for this independent palace and it bit boring place to visit. It's good if you have not scheduled anything just go there and see. Nothing much important to learn and it's all about Architecture and how new president palace built after bombing the old French president house. It's Chinese architecture building and it's really nice architecture. But I feel it's good if you interested in how president palace functioned. It has separate meeting and conference halls and it's has different and unique interior setup. Also it has footage collection of previously ruled presidents and US presidents who visited to Vietnam after they got independent.

Be sure to visit with a guide who can share the history or use the portable electronic guide as available, otherwise you'll just be walking through a building that's a throw back to the 70s. The information available provides a more appreciative context to the building. The bunker area was cool to see.

Only 40,000 vnd but pretty boring. I was expecting to see more about the story behind the independence but all we got to see were the rooms that the president and VIPs used. The bomb shelter was a bit more interesting with some of the old telecommunication equipment. There was an art show happening later in the day there so maybe we were restricted in where we were able to go but I would not waste time here if your visit is short.

It's really cheap to get into, so if you have time it might be nice to see. The place is in dire need of some maintenance and there is little to no information or explanations of anything. The helicopters and tanks in the garden and on the rooftop are cool.

Just a historical relic The 50 years old building is not so special in architecture. It's just a historical relic for the fall down of Saigon in 1975. If you been here in 1990s, you can see more interior equipments much higher value, the dissappeared nowadays. It's quite nice to visit innerside, especially at Embassadors Reception Room. But sometimes you may got disturbed from Chiness tourist groups.

Modern and opulent structure set in a large green space in the center of the city. Your free to wonder about and look in the rooms, both those that were used for governmental activities and some of the living quarters. Other than the replica tank out front I founds it rather lifeless, never got any connection to the events that took place in this building.

I came as part of a tour but this place was my least favourite thing of the whole day. To me it's was just a whole lot of rooms with furniture in them. Sad to see how some were living the good life when those around them where being killed in the war.
Although the park around is calm and lovely with few derelict army motors , not a lot to see unless you like 60/70's décor .
A bit of interesting history but not much to see. I suspect there was a lot that was not open to the public
Don't bother with this trip nothing really to see the best part of this trip was walking back through the gardens

It kind of looks like a average looking hotel from the front and there's not much more to see inside the entrance fee is 40000 the only thing of interest is the army equipment out front but just down the road there's the war museum which is 20 times better value for money go there.

It is probably enough just to read about this place. I found it rather underwhelming and uninteresting. I know its significant to vietnams history and all that but if youre in ho chi minh for a short time i think it isnt worth it.
went to visit this independence palace and found it to be a very boring place with not much to see except some carpets and modern style furniture , not worth the visit .

This "museum" is the site of the old presidential palace preserved in the all its 80s glory. Only the most mundane rooms are open and it's a fine way to spend an hour to say you did it but it's not anything spectacular.
The history of the place is certainly very interesting but the building itself is not particularly grandiose. The grounds are lovely.

This is on all the tour lists but I found it the least interesting. It is not an architecturally interesting building. It housed the last president of South Vietnam. It may be of historical interest to some but you could read about what happened here and not miss anything if you did not go to see it.

A typical drive by photo opportunity as we were not allowed inside. You are in the area so should see it, but don't expect to be blown away by architectural beauty. It was built in concrete in the 60s... As a symbol of the reunification of Vietnam after such a long and brutal war it is VERY symbolic. Therefore a must see.

The Securities sucks! Once u tresspassed a restricted vicinity the securities will follow you everywhere! LMFAO...tho it was my second visit :) lalalaa~ the entrance fee was gettin higher too! back in 2015 it was only VND15,000 and now its VND30,000! fuuuuuhhhhgggg~~~

Nothing impressive. Not a must go if you're on a tight schedule. Tickets are 30k vnd per entry. It was rather crowded when I went around 10am. Only went as it was walking distance from the war remnant musuem.
We saw that 3 days ago .that was a usual bulding with veryyyy simple decoration.only amazing thing in this building was an underground shelter with best facilities in war time.

Culturally significant but very dull Popped in to have a look round as we do understand the significance of this place historically. But it's really just a building with some big rooms in. The tanks and planes outside aren't even the ones that did the deed back in the day, which was disappointing. We didn't stay long, I wouldn't put it at the top of your list.
Went there as part of a schedule tour. Nothing too interesting to see, besides the architecture of the building.

I have to say a rather staid place to visit. It was on a Sunday so really busy with locals visiting. It is a 60's building with some large grandish meeting rooms with large chairs miles apart so no one would actually be able to talk to anyone. It is important to the Vietnamese as it was the seat of government and when it was captured it signalled the end of the Vietnamese war. Again worth a look during the week if possible but only if time allows. Part of a group visit so don't know about price to get in.

Don't bother going in This is a pretty dull place. There is little (any?) information about it's history and it is just a rather dull modern piece of architecture. If you have traveled widely and seen palaces in Europe - this will be a massive disappointment. Kids were bored senseless... so was I.

It really was a big disappointment for us- when walking up to the building from the huge white fence I could not wait seeing it from closer. However, after the fourth or so room with nothing to see apart from some pieces of furniture and oriental tapestry we started to understand that there is nothing more to it. For me, the only interesting room was the one where the old-fashioned bakelit phones that I remember from my childhood were put in line, next to each other, as if time had stopped in the early eighties and someone was just waiting for an emergency call from the Soviet Union or East Germany. Why is this room left like that... We had no idea. To our kids the place was really uninteresting: the six-year-old had some fun in running up and down the wide corridors but the teens were definitely suffering.

The building is obviously steeped in history and is in a great setting, but we were in and out in 15 minutes. It's basically a showcase of all the meeting and conference rooms that were used by previous governments. The bomb shelter in the bottom was the most interesting. If you have time on your hands then it's worth strolling around, otherwise I wouldn't bother.

Quite disappointed to see this palace.the rooms were large but not well decorated etc. Thought that they could have done better on the tour also. The bunker part is quite interesting...... They need to air condition the place up, too hot!

I though this didn't have anything to offer. It has very little furniture (not anything out of the ordinary), little to none English explanations, no content. The documentary offered in the basement is very poor quality and hard to understand. It's a very cheap entrance fee so if you do go you are pretty much only wasting your time.

unable to enter at 11 am I walk all the way from Dragon Palace II hotel to the Palace. Happy to reach there about 11am but it closes from 11am till 1 pm. Unable to enter I went to the opposite small park to sit down to wait till 1 pm. But I was greeted by such annoying shoe cleaner. These people comes one by one first to do some repair to my shoe and cleaning. Then another one come to clean my wife shoe and a third one came to clean my daughter's shoe. AND they charged me exorbitant price of 900000 dong which i can buy a pair of new shoe. Such disgrace to the country.

It was an eye opener initially, however throughout the ?4-5 levels, you will be pretty much seeing the same thing Chairs, arranged around a tables Several President's bedroom that shows bed, wardrobe Basement levels with multiple rooms of radios, telephone and transmitter Not worth a visit unless you are in the vincity It is 5mins walk away from Tao Dan park What is commendable is the cafe within the fence of the independence palace (few minutes walk from the back of the palace), Main course that costs about $400,000 each
Interesting history- poorly run This place is packed and like a rat run. Although there is good information about the place and a good view at the top, i was left feeling unimpressed

For twice the price (30.000)of the war museum this place was icredibly disappointing with very little to see and do. Descriptions of rooms and their significance were good. Recent history so 'palace' felt a strange term as you'll see if go This place similar to the war museum shuts over lunch so arrive before 11.30 or after 13.00. 'Palace' grounds are a nice place to grab a coffee or drink
My friend and i felt that it was not worth the 30k vnd we paid to enter. It was just a display of the bedrooms and meeting rooms. Nothing impressive except the history behind it.
It is not worth visiting this building. There is nothing to see inside. Nevertheless, both the gardens and the "historical meaning" of this building makes it a "must"

Mildly interesting but ugly building and no real need to go inside. This building is little historical significance and is very ugly compared with the palace it replaced. There is not much to see inside and in my view not worth the time or money to go inside. Much better to spend your time at The War Remnants Museum.

We decided to go here based on our guide book and the close location to our hotel. It was only 30,000 VND so not a big expenditure and still glad we went but we probably spent a total of 15 minutes once inside. There is not a lot to see. There's some pretty rooms, furniture, and rugs but that's about it. The entire place is not air conditioned either so it's pretty muggy and stuffy in there, especially in the bunkers downstairs. I wouldn't recommend

This is an important historical site. This was where the head of south Vietnam stayed and also where they signed the surrender to north Vietnam. Nothing so great to see here though. It used to be a very old building, which was bombed and remade.

No aircon, hot heat, just furniture viewing Unless if you really want to read through all the writings, it's basically a showcase of olden times furniture in a non-airconditioned setting. Quite a walk. Nowhere to buy a cold drink so please bring lots of water or else thirst is imminent.

Not worth the entrance fee It's nice to know the history of the country but we feel that the entrance fee of 30,000 VND is quite expensive. All you get to do is roam the place, the rooms are closed and are for viewing only. Not much of a view inside really. The only good scenic view is the water fountain outside the palace. I suggest to skip this place if you're on a budget travel.

No need to visit. Nothing unique about this palace. My family and I found this palace to be quite boring. Nothing unique. Does not reflect the beauty of Vietnam at all (except for the gardens). It feels like it could be anywhre in the world.

If you have seen the palaces in Turkey, then you can totally skip this palace. Not much to see as most areas are not for public viewing. Walking distance from the War Remnants Museum, so you can club a visit to both sites. Visit the War Remnants Museum first and only if you have time visit the palace. We finished touring the palace in a hour's time, as there was not very much to see.
Was disappointing, really not much to see. For a palace it is quite dull. On upside it doesn't cost much to go in.

I'm baffled why this place has such high ratings. Aside from the educational documentary toward the end of our visit, this place bored me to tears. It consisted of a bunch of mediocre rooms. What a waste of time...

We had our own private guide which made this tout some what all right . Otherwise there isn't much to see . It's not really that impressive. Nothing like Versailles in France or Hermitage in Saint- Petersburg Russia . I could easily skip this .

Entry is at 135 Nam Ky Khoi Nghia Street. It is a 5 storey presidential palace of South Vietnam. A replica of Tank #843 which crashed through the gate is parked on the lawn. On display is the recreation room with a circular sofa, the 1960s phones, radios, and office equipment in the basement. A photo gallery finishes the visit. There is an outdoor café on the grounds of the palace. Open daily 0730-2300 hrs. Entry 30,000 dong.

Ghastly 60's archiecture Although we had a guide this doesn't feature in my one of my fondest memories of Ho Chi Minh City. Room after room of chairs and tables. I only found the basement of mild interest.

This was pretty dull really, it can best be described as a large empty building with many rooms full of chairs. And that's about it. If you are a history buff, then it may be more interesting, but for your average tourist I really can't see the appeal. The entry price is cheap, and it takes about an hour to see everything, so no downside risk, just don't expect much, unless you're expecting rooms with chairs, then you expect many!

If you like architecture? If you like architecture then you may like The independence Palace. For me it was just looking at rooms, you could touch anything and there were no " activities". The highlight was going down into the bomb bunker. If I was walking past I may have visited but wouldn't go for an organised tour.
A lot of history about how it has been renamed so many times. The Inside is just a conference hall - the sooner they knock it down and make the area a public garden the better

Boring - but historic Wandered around the palace - along with lots of other 'tourists' - Vietnam has realised that anything that they can slap an 'historic' / 'war' / 'atrocities' label on it will attract the western punters - avaoid
A dull if necessary visit. It represents the beginning of modern Vietnam. Not sure what that means as its changing by the year.

This will not be one of the highlights of anybodies trip to Saigon. The Vietnamese are clearly proud of their history and culture however there is not really much to see of interest here. As my wife commented on one of the rooms "it looks like my 6th form common room"

I didn't enjoy this. It's a short trip from the incredible War Museum so we popped in but it is a waste of an hour wandering around this palace. Students of architecture might get something from it but I didn't.

There are many areas potentially one may miss when visiting. Visitors can go all the way up to the helipad via staircases. Hidden in the underground bunkers are some revelation of operation rooms during wartime. There is even a presidential room down there. Quite an eye opener!
Nothing really impressive about the palace. Few meeting rooms with mediocre design and historical elements. I'd rather spend more time walking around the city than waste money and time on that site.

We were very disappointed when we arrived to visit this attraction. There was a sign up on the ticket window indicating that they were closed for two days. I think it was quite a surprise because two large tour buses were also there at the same time and dint seem to know about this closure. Not sure of the reason, but it seems that they are allowed to do without notifying anyone about a closure.

I'm not sure if was because some areas were under construction, but I found this attraction to be extremely dull. There are few artefacts on display and what you do see if akin to looking at a few dull rooms with hotel like furniture. The most interesting part was the bar room on the top floor. Also, the documentary films are grindingly dull and we actually started to doze off during one of them. The architecture is interesting and this place seems popular but if you have limited time, you can skip it without missing much.
We found it quite boring on the whole however the guide did share info that helped us to understand a bit more of Vietnams history but didn't really rock my boat!

Not much to see inside I just visited the Palace and I thought it would have been better to just sit in the garden right in front of it and take pictures instead of going inside. I didn't think there is anything interesting on the inside.

A waste of time and money I was looking forward to the bunker but it's closed until 12th or 16th Jan'16 for renovations! The 3 (or 5) floors above ground level are nothing great. It has dining and function rooms, etc. I regret spending more time at the palace and less at the War Remnants Museum.

Don't expect a palace here... it is only a building with large rooms and hallways. If you are a modern history and War buff you will still enjoy looking into the large rooms where the President and officers sat, had meetings, hosted parties and planned to kill many while looking into the big maps during the 60s. The only rather interesting point is the helicopter on the rooftop. All the other things, like the Mercedes car the President used et all are just about ok. Nothing spectacular.

Dull and uninspiring, especially for someone born decades after Vietnam was reunited. We went without a guide and found the information around the palace sparse. One could wish that the exhibits would be made more interactive to convey the historical importance of events that took place here, but the building seems to be frozen in time.

It looks cool from the outside but once u get inside u'll know the struggle. Not much things there, just some rooms like gaming room, movie theater room, etc & u're not allow to go inside the room just standing outside & observe

This is a palace with icons and architecture from the 60's that have been preserved. If you are interested in history, then this is the place for you. It also offers a great view of Ho Chi Minh from the palace's roof top. The entrance fee is a bit pricy for what it offers.

In need of work, dirty rugs, really dusty. Too many tourist buses, haggled by hawkers on the way. Might have been good years ago but now it looks dated. Didn't take long to walk around. The grounds/greenery was the only good looking items around the building. Definitely wouldn't call it a palace.
We went there expecting a great museum...nothing special, rooms are not very interesting, nothing really special, do worth the visit or time.

Here is an example of a building frozen in 1975 when South surrendered to the North and the Vietnam war was over. While it is an interesting walk through the building and bunkers from where the offensive was led, the building is an extreme waste of great space. Such massive underutilization is sad to see. A few rooms could be preserved, but the entire building does not justify the cost. Much more impressive on the outside. Ok to visit if you are more than 3 days in Saigon.

Boring and not worth the money All the rooms are very similar, multiple levels of the same stuff, the only interesting thing was the bunker which not many people would have seen before, was exciting to see life without the current technology and advancements, with a high use of maps and basic telecommunication, if your wanting to clock up some steps and become bored this is the place for you
Very plain with mainly rooms for dinner parties, bedrooms, meeting rooms and the best is the basement with the old communications equip.
Just look at it from the outside This building looks great from the outside so just take a photo and leave it at that. Nothing much to see inside but big conference rooms and empty spaces

Film footage of a tank smashing through the gates of the palace is exciting. the building is not. Frusty typical austere rooms abound with no elegance or reference to their political relevance. I hear that there is an original wireless room, but have never sen it.
And example of a local historical land mark from the war. A little underwhelming. Close to the centre of town in district 1.

The environment has no issue with me which is great. A nice place for taking lot of beautiful photos. However, I do not understand why there is no air-conditioner here, so the trip to here is hot, hot and hot!

Not essential if pushed for time This is ok as it is cheap (£1 entry fee) but is just a series of rooms with a small information board outside each and a velvet rope across the door. The top floor is worth a visit for a good view down the street but otherwise I would recommend going if you are pushed for time.

Tour guide made it boring The building is amazing! The history involved the the layout is beyond belief (in terms of design and purpose). I didn't enjoy it much though I think largely because of the tour guide company we used. They were very boring and draining. Otherwise, definitely worth a visit. Can be visited alone as every room has a sign translated in English which explains it.

Best thing is it is cheap Basically you pay 30k to see a bunch of furniture. Don't go there close to lunch because then the staff will more or less throw you out, either go as it opens or after 2 p.m. Abit less propaganda than other museums in vietnam but it is still a pinch of it and ot conflicts with what you know from western history lessons, so take it both lightly! Nice house

We really enjoyed our visit here but sadly not for what we saw! Our guide was fantastic and managed to make even this very dull building fun. There is little to make it feal like a Place other than the green space in a mad city.

Lovely building with lots of history but it's essentially full of different rooms describing their purpose with a different fancy table with some different fancy chairs in each one. The highlight was the bunker which feels more real and has more history to it

Couldn't have been more bored or unimpressed with this "palace". Basically a 1970's building with lots of rooms with chairs in. We left half way round as it was a massive waste of time. You can learn everything you need to know from the far more interested War remnants museum.

If you enjoy architecture, you might like this... The palace looks impressive but for me, thats a far as it went. I found it boring. All we seemed to do was peer in at rooms that had fancy tables or chairs. While I know it is an important part of the history of the country, I feel your time would be best spent in other mueseums in the area that animate the countrys history in a more thought provoking way

If you like conference rooms, you'll love this place! It's an office building. Nothing more, nothing less. Yes, things happened here 50 years ago, but there is absolutely nothing to show it. Ooh, a room with a desk. Wow- a room with a table. There are some replica tanks and planes. Oh, there's a small bar on the rooftop if you're so inclined. (I'm not) Seriously- there is absolutely nothing of substance here, but I'll give it two stars as this empty shell at least had some shade.
Rectangular rooms with furniture set up in a rectangle on every floor. Having said that, admission is low and it's right in the middle of the city so no reason not to walk through if you're there
Only stopped there on our bike tour from the outside. We did not visit the inside. Historical value, but architecture not interesting
