Unless you have a need to see any and everything with some connection to the US/Vietnam war, don't waste your time. This essentially a government style office building. A visitor can observe various large meeting rooms (from the outside) as well as the presidential family quarters (from the outside), and some "war rooms" with wall maps used by the US and South Vietnamese command structure. Go to one of the many cafes and relax instead.

Enclosed, Not much ventilation There is not a whole lot to see. The place could have been maintained a bit better internally. Most rooms are enclosed and you would feel trap with no ventilation, no AC. I am not sure whether the elevator I working. I saw several people had a hard time to go upstairs and they have to stop on the middle of the staircase to catch some breath.

I like museums but I wasn't that impressed by this one. It just lacked something. It wasn't engaging. Perhaps I don't know enough about the history and this could be why. But there was just various room with small signs that you needed to read. There was a tv downstairs playing some information but felt they could have done it differently. Had the times that it would start and end with a big screen. It just seemed that you could walk in whenever.

Go fo the basement, nothing else Only the basement of this palace is interesting for those who wants to understand how the military operations happened in april 1975 and few days before the fall of Saigon. The rest are rooms decorated from the seventies without any interest at all. The propaganda film at the end is quite funny though. Only go there if you really do not know what to do in Saigon...
I found it very boring. It's like walking through a sauna. Lots of big rooms with furniture in it. To me, it looked very staged. Only interesting thing was the bunker room.

Maybe just because it was such a hot day, but, it was very underwhelming. Our guide was good and informative on all aspects, it's just not that interesting looking at old rooms with smelly old furniture. You can get a nice cool,drink,from upstairs stairs though.
Didn't think much of this - interesting to see the tanks etc. in the grounds but the palace itself is pretty boring. Wouldn't bother going to see unless you have time on your hands.

Really not much to see here, apart from the luxurious lifestyle of a poor ( at the time of construction) nations president. It is a beautiful building with outstanding gardens. The highlight is the bunker and the roof lounge/ night club.

I was excited with the initial view of walking into this place given the obvious history. However, after an hour or so into the tour, I found the exhibits and rooms boring and not so interesting. The fee for this attraction is obviously low but the state of this place is rather run down and the exhibits and rooms not too exciting. This is a place I can simply scratch off my 'bucket list' but I won't be returning any time soon in the future.

And full of Chinese tourist. Not like there's anything wrong with Chinese tourist, but I think only communists nations get the whole communist palace kind of thing, because this place was BEYOND boring!!! Oh look this is where the President sat (in like seven rooms we saw this), this is where he ate, and slept, and danced, and hide from bombs dropping overhead. Seriously, go to an art museum, a bar, heck the garden/park across the street was more interesting.
Palace built in the 1960's. Pretty plan Jane and not that interesting. It was part of our all day tour of the city. I think it was more of a gap filler to eat up time.
Just a big building of the 60's with many rooms. Nothing interesting unless you have a link/history in the Vietnam civil war of the north and south.

We were disappointed, although I understand that this is a highly rated stop on the tourist trail. Basically, there were lots of formal rooms, set up as they were 40 years ago. In the basement there were more rooms, bombproof, where the war was coordinated. We were back out the door in 40 minutes.

This is really nothing special, a big building with big rooms, very simple and not really exciting. You can safely skip it, unless of course you are very interested in an old government building that is used today for wedding parties and occasional official events.
This place bored me to tears! I wasn't that enthused about seeing luxurious rooms where politicians meet. Nice French influence architecture though.

Not a palace but 60s gov building. We visited this location and were disappointed. not the original palace which was french .. but a new 60s construction with 60s furniture..do not recommend to visit, waste of time. pic from outside would have been sufficient.
hmmm.... dont know what to say I really dont see the reason to visit this place as it is only rooms with chairs and tables in 3 floors. nothing i would recommend.
What I liked...nothing! It was a bunch of meeting rooms that meant nothing to me. Lots of floors and stairs and it was extremely hot in the building.
Just a lot of meeting rooms, so what???????????????????????. Save your time and money on the entrance fee.
the bunker was the only part I thought was interesting. The rest is a 1960's styled building which is all furnished with ornate furniture. Honestly I was so disappointed I wasted my time.

Better buy a book and learn aboutthis place by yourself as this place will not give you any details of who what when and why. Very poor historic place which does not give the honor to such an important place for Vietnam.

Only if you have extra time I would suggest skipping the palace unless you have nothing else to do. The only interesting facet were the bunkers where several maps from the 1970s were posted showing troop locations and movement. Otherwise, you can wander around the corridors and look at a lot of poorly decorated rooms. Very little information presented and our guide didn't have much to add either.
As usual with any palace the lawns are nice and the building is beautiful. But even if you miss this attraction it's fine as not that great.
I expect a palace to look like a palace, not an anonymous concrete block. It won't feature in many photo albums.

What a disappointment! Our guide bristled with pride as she showed us round this concert block. The original palace had been built in the French Colonial style by the French – obviously – but had been destroyed by the Vietnamese and re built, what a shame It reminded me somewhat of a council building in inner London. There are several rooms you can poke your head in to and a few items of beautiful furniture and carpets; along with the bunkers. My husband and I love sightseeing and visiting interesting buildings but if you are short of time don’t worry about missing this!
There were many reception halls but the grandeur of a palace was lacking. Seemed more like a modern hotel. Can't even say a luxurious hotel.
pretty mundane - just rooms of congress and so forth. for the price of entry in comparison to other sights, i wasn't that fussed.

This "Palace" has to be the most boring looking building to be given such a title. The 60's architecture can be found in most municipal council buildings in the UK. The history behind the Palace is far more interesting than the lump of stone you walk around. The endless square rooms are characterless. The iconic entrance gate & Tanks are the only saving grace. Read up on the history and don't waste precious time on the carbuncle itself.

You can just take some pictures from the gate and that's it.... Inside you will see nothing worthed... It's an old building with stairs and after the tour - at least I tought - Ok I came here but would not did if missed...

Lied about opening times We got there towards 4 and thought it was too late, but were informed outside that it was open till 5. The ticket office confirmed that we had till 5 to explore, so we brought our tickets. We explored the basement and the ground floor rooms and were kicked out by the guard at 4.25pm. So about 40 minutes - not very good value, but we don't feel we missed much from what we did see. Frankly, its best purpose was to be under a roof during a HCMC afternoon downpour.....

Typical Socialistic Palace Boring place. A palace that you can see in any post-soviet country. Same design as everywhere, same message. And not much history on display. The only thing I really like in the museum is the garden outside.

Just a Government building Visited this "Palace" a few days ago as part of an organised trip and found it disappointing. There are rooms may have hosted important meetings many years ago but they are now virtually empty, just a table or a desk. May be of interest to those keen to visit landmark places associated with the Vietnam war, but to me it was very far from a Palace.

While this palace gives an insight into presidential life in the 1960s, it has little else to offer. The architecture is unappealing (neither modernistic or historic). View from the roof toward the city was pleasantly green. Small souvenir shop on ground floor has some interesting photos of historic events at the palace. Entry fee over-priced compared to War Remnants Museum.

Not too sure how this gets to the top of places to see and do in HCMC but it was very boring. Cost about 30,000 Dong to enter which was fine but there was not much to see. Not recommended to thrill-seekers etc, nothing to do here... move along.
This is an odd one, really not worth a visit in my opinion, used to look very attractive in days of old, then it was bombed, this unfortunate concrete eyesore now replaces the original.

Not really interesting for us It's more of a showroom than anything actually. It was incredibly hot on the day we visited and no air conditioning was available anywhere in the palace so that didn't help at all. It contains lots of history and antique showrooms but nothing really interesting or memorable. If you like interior design and love looking at showrooms then this would be a place for you to visit, but for me and my family, it was nothing special. Paid around 30-50k VND to visit and decided to leave after an hour. I would recommend this as a third choice kind of place to visit. If you're short of time, visit the more interesting places like the Post office or HO CHi Minh square before coming to this one.
Visited the reunification palace after the war museum and god it was bored in comparison! You get what you pay for. The film they played downstairs was the only interesting part.
Best to avoid it. We just did it because of the time we had already booked in HCMC. Better off to sit in a bar and drink

Yes, it of historical interest but hardly worth a visit. 2 tanks that burst through the gates on display, a couple of old cars (a Merc and a Jeep) in the basement (airless). A lot of abandoned typewriters and communication equipment, also in basement. Upstairs a delegation room (table and chairs), a reception room (table and chairs), the presidential room (table and chair), a cinema room (rows of chairs), dining room (table and chairs), assorted other rooms (tables and chairs). So, unless you are into period furniture give it a miss.

If we could do it again we would skip this and go straight to the War Remnants Museum. Not only is the Reunification Palace twice the price, it was just really overrated and boring. I am a big history fan so this was disappointing.

Not attractive inside or out Very modern, looked more like a school/hospital than a palace. Wasn't really any more interesting inside! If you have nothing to do you may as well go, as it is so cheap, but not worth going out of your way for.

This was a waste of our time. It is very 70s European architecture and quite frankly a number of rooms with some stately furniture in. My 9 yr old, would rather have put pins in his eyes, especially after a long day touring Cu Chi tunnels! Drive past would have been more than enough for us.

Although perhaps interesting for history buffs, I found it rather unremarkable, and, could I say, even boring. There are about 5 floors, comprised mostly of meeting and conference rooms, with mostly insipid interiors. I found the lowest floor, with the photo and video archives, the most interesting. It's probably enough to just walk past and take a photo from the exterior.

You will not miss anything by missing this place i visited as part of my walk around the whole city looking at the attractions. This was the least interesting part. The original building was lost in the war and this was rebuilt by a bad 60's french designer. Cheap to get into tour guides are not needed as just not enough to learn about. All the rooms are closed off so you can only poke your head through a door and is a little big/confusing so easy to get lost.
very poor design. looks like house of someone from 60-s. But the entrance is very cheap. As far as I remember 10 000 - 15 000 dongs.

Former south Vietnam Palce You can skip this unless you want to see the life of president and the tanks that come in to make south Vietnam surrender. There's two lifts, one at the entrance and the other one at the end. take it to the highest floor then slowly walk back down. There's a little cafe outside just opposite the entrance, have a break there and listen to Vietnamese music instrument playing.

You could give this one a pass Not so much a palace, but a drab concrete building with large banquet halls and meeting rooms. I'm usually not that negative about attractions like this overseas, but this one could be skipped without really missing anything. A quick read on Wikipedia would give you about as much information as you would need. Maybe I am the exception to the general opinion here, but I enjoyed other attractions more than this. Tip: The reunification palace is close to Notre Dame Cathedral, linked together by a pleasant park to walk in, so if you are in the area and have some time, it could be a good thing to swing by and check out.
Read about it instead of visiting it There were lines waiting to get inside this really ugly, contemporary building, a monument to bad taste. Read about it and don't waste your time.

If you like to see banquet halls, meeting rooms, and private offices of officials this is OK. The building and park surrounding are OK, but you won't spend much time here. I did not see any elevators so plan on walking stairs.

60's Soviet style concrete block This is no "palace". There was an old palace built by the french colonists, which was demolished and replaced by a typical late sixtes soviet style horizontal block. Inside you can see the meeting rooms where the Saigon surrender was signed. Good for historians.

A 70's building with rooms of 70's opulence But its another pointless thing to see in Saigon, it really is just a 70's building with many rooms with dining tables, sofas and chairs in. If you find that sort of thing exciting then this is the place for you. Personally it didn't hold much interest for me. Its rammed with people all looking through door ways into rooms. I think you'd be better reading a leaflet on the place and giving it a miss.
Interesting but not nearly as good as it could have been The reunification palace or independence palace was interesting as a place but disappointing in terms of the visitor experience.

I never got to go in. But it looked interesting from the outside, and has been basically untouched since the gates were barricaded in the 70's. There was a meeting going on all day so I never got in. Shame.

A bunch of rooms with a bunch of furniture. Nothing interesting and this house is only 40 years old. Do not waste time. No history there apart from the pictures that they put on the ground floor of the house.
Check open times with locals not media. We attempted to see this attraction twice within the open times posted and each time the gates were closed and we didn't get the chance to get inside.

Not a lot to see inside, but worth a quick visit. There is a video room in the basement that has an interesting documentary about the history of the building, and a room next to it with pictures and descriptions of the evolution of the building. We couldn't get upstairs.

A very plain concrete building, but ok it has history You stare through the railings at this building and your guide will tell you it's dramatic history and point out the tank but there are so many other things to do in the city I am not sure that apart from the photo that it's worth a visit. There was a painting exhibition when we were there.

The reunification palace looked grand from outside. Nice fountain in the lawn. However, there was hardly anything interesting Inside the building and everything was "cold" and without much character. Considering everything was preserved as it was in 1960s, the architecture, furniture and items seemed "dated" rather than vintage. There was no air conditioning and the afternoon heat was not so pleasant and conducive for exploring the place.There were a few standing fans but there was little relief from the afternoon heat. All I could remember was many big rooms with big tables and many chairs and I soon lost my enthusiasm to explore the place. Perhaps, if there was a guided tour, it could have been more interesting for me so I could gain the perspective from a local who shares Vietnam's history with passion and not rely on visual impact.

I wish i had not gone I wish i had not gone....to be fair i am not a big fan of buildings etc, and particularly grand governmental ones ..... but this was a wasted trip....did not feel it was educational or added to the time in Ho Chi Minh. enter if you have a personal interest, otherwise there is much else to see in HCMC

A huge building, frozen in time. Walking around you see meeting room, after meeting room, after meeting room, with nothing but period furniture in them. Then in the back there's the residence where you see the bedrooms, the family room, and the movie room. Unless you book a guided tour, there's nothing that explains anything about these rooms, so it's hard to understand the historical connections here.

This place did not appeal to me at all. To me it's just a building. It does have its significance and some rooms were nice looking and I know some important decisions were made there. Personally, I didn't like it.
Not much to see. The tanks and the fighter jet on the campus are not the originals, but the replicas or the same type. The building is mostly empty and croweded and stuffy.

Entry is cheap, the palace is large and there is a lot of walking to be done, but there isn't much to read and the only draw of this place is the awe of the palaces size and the grandness of it's furnishings.
Better far much better is the war museum. HCMC doesn't have a lot to offer for cultural or history places, and this is probably why this place is considered a landmark.

Adult fee 30k and kids 3k Noting much inside except looking at some function rooms, gambling room and ballroom with a cinema below. It was a hot and humid afternoon so it's pretty uncomfortable. Just an old building but the front is nice tough with a big green field and a fountain. If you don't take an English guide you won't understand much of e history. We didn't take as they say must wait for a big group before they conduct the tour. We are feeling to sweaty to wait. 30k is expensive if you compare to War Remnants museum Just a see see look look venue less than an hour.

Unless you really have nothing to do and go for the guided tour, it can be pretty boring here. The film gives a good insight, but that's about it. Or maybe it's just my level of interest.. I dunno. Just felt it wasn't an awesome must visit.
Not much to see, just four floors of rooms and the lift was broken!!! The grounds were quite pretty though. Its also more expensive than we thought it was going to be...

In case you are not aware, this is basically the old White House equivalent for the South Vietnamese. Since it was evacuated in the 70s, not much has changed. For that reason, you get the experience of walking through a dated old building with random chairs scattered about different rooms. There just isn't much to see here, and the free tour they offer doesn't provide much more additional insight. Probably the most entertaining thing was the basement area where they showed a few strategic military maps and things. But other than that, you're literally walking from almost empty room to almost empty room, "And this is where the President entertained his guests"..."And in the next room, this is where guests waited before speaking with the president"..."And this is the kitchen"...etc., etc.
Don't go - very boring Not worth the entry fee. Nothing but a lot of rooms with 70s furniture. Only interesting thing to see was the helicopter on the roof.
Take a photo from the outside, otherwise it is like every 1970s town hall that you will find in the UK, except with a helicopter on the roof.

I never get guides but I wish we got one here as we had no idea what we were looking at. It looks like a movie set from the 80s but you don't really know what you are looking at or the history. The information in the basement should be moved to the top so you can get an appreciation at the beginning. It is only then that you know who came here and what it was used for. You can usually make up for 'boring' trips with photos but, again, it was just like a set off a movie. If you are a historicity buff you may like it. If you are on limited time give this a skip.

Uninspiring and Boring! Honestly??? This is not a palace by any means - just a large government office building - where you are shown meeting room after meeting room and none of them are interesting or even pretty to look at. Who hasn't seen a meeting room before? The much praised tunnels are blocked off so you don't actually get to see them! Avoid unless you are really keen on meeting rooms and offices ...
All they did (form what I can see) is change "Presidential" to "reunification" Palace. There is not a lot to see, mainly just walk around old empty rooms as they supposedly were when Saigon fell.

This means more to them than to us foreigners. Design is obviously not to everyone's taste being art deco I suppose but it's very limited in its contents and explanations so you can whizz round not really understand ing too much of who was there and why and when etc. A guide book is a must and its all cheap to get in etch so worth a visit if you have an hour to waste.
An awful building in the worst of modernist taste. There is nothing to see here, and the film is propaganda.
Unless you are Vietnamese or a historian, then skip it. Nothing more than a building with empty chairs for Westerners.

I don't understand the appeal of this place. We toured this place on our visit to Saigon. It was very inexpensive to get in but I don't really understand the tourist appeal here. It felt like a tour of an abandoned office building - complete with the circa 1970 furnishings.

It was the first stop on our afternoon tour and wished we never stopped. Yes the building looks cool from the outside with the two tanks and helicopter on the rooftop but...THAT'S IT!!! You are walking into a home that was taking over through a foreclosure. There is older furniture, a pool table with no pockets, a bar and a few conference rooms. I do not understand why you would spend more then 15 minutes there.

Not the highlight of my stay I like to integrate visits to historical sites when visiting a place since it provides more depth to the place you are visiting. That said I did not find the Reunification Palace of much interest. I know that it once functioned as a political centre but it really just feels like a building with various meeting rooms. The communications centre in the basement with much of the antiquated equipment was probably the most interesting part of the visit, but was sufficient for me to justify visiting the place.

Boring place with no surprise There are nothing special here except old furnitures and equipment. The explanation onsite is lacking as well. Mostly still related to the french and war. Skip this place, browsing the photos heremight be enough.

Worth maybe an hour but three blocks up Lệ Duan St, at Kumhoe Plaza, are two of the best restaurants in town--Bobby CHINN'S and Bassilico. Afterward you can go back 1block and visit the beautiful main Post Office and the Notre Dame Cathedral.

A Waste of Time and Money Thank goodness it's not an expensive entry fee. This has to be one of the tediously boring palaces in the world! If you're idea of fun is looking at extremely large meeting and dining rooms from a roped off doorway, then this is for you. Even the underground bunker area has nothing of interest save a few old maps on the walls. If you're in Ho Chi Minh City and have time on your hands, go watch some grass grow in the nearby park - it will be more interesting.

The story itself is impressive, but not the building . Not much to see over there and you can here the complete story of you have a good guide on one of your other tours, for example towards the tunnels
Quite honestly all you see is a room with chairs and a table where the peace treaty was signed, or did I miss something?

The palace is really not what I expected at all, it's a fairly large 1960 building with nothing inside. There are numerous rooms but they only have very minimal furniture inside and could be almost anywhere. The only part of the palace worth visiting are the bunkers which are more interesting but unless you have a lot of time in HCMC I wouldn’t bother
a modern building in a prime lovely setting. Furnished in lavish modern style. Should not be viewed in the western sense of a museum. just normal government accommodation.

Wow - another room, empty but for a desk with an old phone on it. So... there are a couple of old tanks outside in the grounds. And there is a chopper on the roof. Sadly, at least visually, the fun stops there. Almost every room is the same. Everyone was peering in through the doorways; hoping to see something that might divulge some historical significance. But nothing. Nothing but another desk which, except for an antique telephone, was bare. The place is stuck in the 1970s - that's true. But it's a very sanitized representation of what was obviously there. It looks like it has been stripped of everything that may have had a bit of history attached to it. Other reviews seem to suggest that people have got a lot more out of their visit to this place when they've been taken around by a tour guide, so, possibly that's where I went wrong. Their is an elevator, but it's small and slow. There is no air conditioning throughout the building. The biggest failing of this place is the lack of information for visitors. There is very little to read about the palace as your wander around. Personally, I wish I'd given this place a miss.
Woeful partly because it was closed off as there was some event happening but really there is not a great deal to see. Probably worth 30 minutes at most.
Good History Too Crowded It was a good place actually. But too crowded to see more inside. The security guy wasn't that friendly. I hardly enjoyed this place although I'd like to spent more time here
Normal government building Nothing special about this, looks like a normal government building to me with heavy fences and guards.

I thought this was kind of boring. We went there and bought a book telling us about what things were. It wasn't very detailed, so I wouldn't buy it again. Maybe if we had gone on a tour with a guide it would have been better.

Lots of empty rooms, not much explanations We went there to "kill" time while waiting for the water puppet show. Overall it was very uninteresting. Not much to see and very little in terms of explanations. Many empty rooms, or rooms with 1 desk and 1 old rotary phone. Only good thing was the tanks and the fighter plane on the lawn.

Yes, the history of Vietnam is fascinating and there are many ways to discover it but this isn't the place. It seems a guided tour is the way to do it but we chose to wander around and I ended up with a lot of photos of a rather dull building. It's kinda like being on the set of the Russian headquarters in a 60's James Bond movie but it's really more interesting because of what happened there, rather than what the building has to offer. It's a slightly creepy step back in time and I certainly wouldn't put it on your must see list. The park over the road is much more fun buying coconuts from the gorgeous ladies and sipping them in the shade of a tree.
Don't wast much time seeing the Reunification Palace. There are a few meeting rooms but not much else.
This is definitely not worth seeing. the top floors are boring and the only thing of interest are the rooms in the basement where the communication equipment is located
It turned out to be Independence Palace, and according to the City Guide, the basement set up like a war time movie set was not to be missed. It either didn't exist, or wasn't easily found.
If you like looking at cordoned off rooms then go, but if you like a bit more than rooms it is not that exciting. The fountain out the front is lovely.

There's Better Things To Spend Your Time In Saigon On The Reunification Palace was a 2 1/2 hours stop on a day tour that was 2 hours and 15 minutes too long. The "museum"looks and feels if someone shuttered a building and then reopened it 4 decades later. Other then a helicopter parked on the roof, signifying the US evacuation efforts and the subterranean communications bunker, there not a lot to keep ones attention in regards to the exhibits.
Dont bother is no good. Very boring only furniture to see and little more. The only good things are the bunker command centre under the palace and a chopper helicopter parked in the roof.
Boring. Lots of big empty rooms. Not much to see. There are free tour guides available and included in the price of admission, but our guide was very hard to understand.

Living in Saigon, we have received tonnes of visitors - we have also taken guests to the reunification Palace - to much dissapointment of most visitors and us. Saigon has so much more to offer, so unless you are here for a week, maybe 5 days, we recommend you dont bother visiting this place. It is a piece of history and for those really interested in this - should go, but if you are here for a few days, to experience the hustle and bustle of Saigon, the food, culture - stay away. The palace is essentially just a collection of very large rooms, safety chambers etc., in very Soviet/Russian style. Apart from imagining how gates were broken down, how cause of history was changed, it doesnt offer anything spectacular. This is a memory of different times. If you must have a piece of history, visit War remnants museum... It is afterall better.

The Reunification Palace (or Presidential Palace or Independence Palace, depending on who you're talking to) is probably the "ugliest" palace you will ever see in your life! Consisting of nothing more than one single, concrete monstrosity of a building, it tends to remind one of a bomb shelter. It is in the typical brutalist style so common and in vogue during the 60s and might have been inspired by the leading architectural style in the United States, which is no surprise, given South Vietnam's closeness to the US. Still, it just makes you gag, and you go in wondering, what? why?? how??? Admission prices are not cheap, considering what you are getting, not to mention, of course, the rudeness of the government employed admissions staff. Moreover, they don't even have set hours! They say they will be back from lunch at 1:30, but people showed up at 1:30 to buy tickets, and not a single soul was found! The decor inside is also minimalist, with little more than a furniture piece here, a furniture piece there, and red or green carpet (of the industrial type). There was not even a hint of traditional Vietnamese architecture or neoclassical architecture for that matter. No aesthetic value, whatsoever. All it was was a block of concrete several stories high with windows cut out and a helicopter launch pad at the top. A purely utilitarian building (though it even failed to live up to its main purpose, which was to safely house the president of South Vietnam during the war). My friend basically wanted to get out the minute she had set foot in the building. I was at least better, wanting to tour all the halls first. But then, when I went into the movie room, and all I could see playing was anti-American propaganda, I was like "Really? Are they THAT desperate? Even though the war's been over for 30-40 years? Who are they trying to kid?" That was when I nearly threw up! So other than the tanks at the front of the palace, nothing much else to see. Good to visit just to say "Here is where it all started and ended." But that's it!

You pay a small entrance fee and can watch a video clip and go on a guided tour of the Presidential Palace & former seat of govt of the South Vietnam govt. Its quite boring being shown rooms,banquet halls etc and we left half way thru the tour.

Not quite what I was expecting. Well the palace is big but I dont think there is much to see there. It's quite boring for me. And when you go during peak period, its gonna be so HOT and packed! There is no air con in there. You'll also have to walk up and down the stairs to view the different sections because the elevators are so so so SMALL! it can only fit like 5 ppl ><

On arrival the building looked like a school. Inside was not much better. The furniture was well preserved making it look modern, and alot of new things like carpets had been added. There are many replicas used in the building which was disappointing. We were expecting more architecture and design when the word 'palace' is used. Fee is 30,000VND ($1.5)

there really isn't much to see or do. the building has little character and the place is quite empty of anything worth visiting take a pic with you of the helicopters evacuating people from the roof at the end of the vietnam war and it provides a little more fizz

if you hope to read something in every part of the building and get to know about the palace on your own, you are in a wrong place. Just try to get a guide or join secretely to some group, otherwise you will be just watching boring furniture from 60s. To see the tanks and helicopter closely you can just head to the War remants museum right away. For 30.000d it is overpriced, just compare with incredible War museum full of informations. The palace was full of school groups on Saturday (also other museums full of young students - be aware)

Badly in need of restoration and care This building was a non event.... It is badly in need of restoration and repair of building, furniture and decoration. It is a shame as it represents an important time in recent history.

remembered for the moment not the building rather like something from an austin powers film ? nice large grounds , the palace itself could do with a good clean , and not saying dancing girls but a bit more effort with the tourist "attraction" label would be nice .
Boring. The biggest attraction? See the gate the tanks plowed through when Saigon fell. Wow.

The stories are interesting, but you don't have to go inside to appreciate them. The furniture is obviously old, and it's no big deal to see where the President sat, slept and entertained foreign officials. It was a waste of time, but part of the tour.

A proud symbol of Vietnam's Progress This sad palace was designed in the 1970's by a Vietnamese grand prix award winning architect architect to replace the destroyed french palace that stood on its site. It's odd 1970's style furnishings do not create much of a spectacle for palace visitors. It is not as captivating as it could be but worth visiting at the price.

Most of what you can see here can be seen from the outside, without paying the 30,000 or whatever the cover is. The history lesson was nice, but if you do your wikipedia first you'll have all the info. The underground bunker was the only interesting part not viewable from the street.
Limited area for tourist viewing Every second floor is open for tourist viewing, with very limited areas on those floors. Worth a look, but a quick one.
this was a bit dissapointing, its not really much of a palace and its very underwelming. we got a free english tour which was nice, otherwise we would have had no clue what anything was

I thought that this would have lots of exhibits and information related to Vietnam's reunification and independence. Instead, it's a walk around a largely run down, dated and musty smelling residence. Highly disappointing.
Skip it. Like a large hotel building. Very little overall of interest to see in Saigon. Feel like a walking dollar sign.

Looks like salford town hall! Really not much to see here, some rooms in the basement looked origional war time but on the most part just looked like a posh conference hall. Good to fill an hour and only 30,000 entry so why not. Wouldnt go again.

Not really worth a visit - you can see it all by walking past There are two interesting parts of the Reunification Palace. 1) It was highly significant in the final days of the Vietnam War when the northern tanks drove through the gate. 2) It was preserved almost in tact from that date - so the interior an amusing 1960s flashback.

Go there if you have a couple of hours to burn. Otherwise, you don't lose much just viewing the building from the main gate. If you are interested in stately buildings of the 60s or 70s era, go stay at the Grand Hotel in Taipei instead.

Vietnam clearly capitalizes on its "Vietnam War" era as far as creating tourist sites. I suggest skipping this place as there really is little of interest to see. The place is frozen in 1968-70. Go to the War Remnants usuem, but as for this place, use your time more wisely.

Boring and very warm, has no air con. The palace has no air con at all, which makes it a stuffy experience in the very hot city. There is no working lift or elevator, so you have to climb up stairs, the building is about 4 storey high. The rooms look untouched from the 70s. There is nothing interesting, since every country has its own government palace grounds. Don't bother with this attraction.

the palace...you can hardly call it that...is absolutely sad and dont bother visiting this place unless you're bored to high heaven. the palace has a huge garden and thats about it...they have a dining room and a meeting room used by the politicians in the olden day and in the basement they have radio rooms/bed rooms etc used during the war. its absolutely plain, not facinating and is not worth the visit or the entrance fee.
This is a historic but modern building. Still worth a walk around. Don't miss out on the military equipment in the basement area.

Interesting testimony of a past period Interesting testimony of a bygone time, of historical events that today, rebuilt, is a government representative building in late sixties style. Suggestive park and view from the building on the wide avenue. Automatically translated

Interesting, But Not Unmissable The Independence Palace, also known as Reunification Palace, was the former home of South Vietnam’s president and the site where a North Vietnamese tank famously crashed through the gates in 1975, marking the end of the Vietnam War—though honestly, with its retro decor, vast meeting halls and bunker, it felt more like a conference venue from yesteryear than a former royal residence. The entry fee is cash only.

you can look at - but no must see The palace is next to the museum. Admission costs 65,000 dong. Elevators are available. The palace is spread over 4 floors. More of a maze to me. You can visit up to the roof and also a bunker. There are different rooms to see - from meeting rooms, an in-house cinema to the command center. A few cars are still on display. As well as tanks. For me, the visit was not super interesting - it is just a palace. Automatically translated

A Must Do, with good audio guide but generally not very exciting or intetesting unless you're interested in interpreting the meaning of decor colour & architecture. No significant info re war cos it's the south except the famous press photograph

Very big, easy to get lost, even with the map. Enjoyed the spy part right down the bottom. Loved looking at the old vehicles but overall we did not so much enjoy it. Liked the tanks outside and yes the place is impeccably kept.

I felt the history of Vietnam. I've been to Ho Chi Minh City a few times, but this was my first visit. There were quite a few other tourists. Anyway, I walked around inside! And it was hot!! A local friend explained everything to me as I looked around. The former South Vietnamese presidential palace, the site of the end of the Vietnam War. A symbolic building of Vietnamese unification. On the rooftop, the equipment used by the president and a helicopter are on display. There was also a tank used by the Liberation Army in a storm in the garden on the premises. It was hard to believe that this happened decades ago.
Educational and Beautiful Grounds Went here as part of a larger tour stop. Beautiful grounds and educational tour. Give yourself time to see the inside and outside of the building.
Visited here on Dec-27,2023 and explored the various rooms inside and saw the central fountain and furniture too.

Skip if on a tight schedule A completely mediocre and forgettable experience. I would only come here if you really have nothing else to do in Saigon. The independence palace is the former seat of government of southern Vietnam, and it has now been turned into a museum. The explanations are fairly lackluster, and the exhibitions do not seem well taken care of. When we were there, the magazine Elle was setting up for a fashion show. This was both interesting and intrusive, as the entire time there were buzz saws going and it was very loud.
The audio tour does contain some general history about the late 70s, which I found more interesting than the building. It was very busy with lots of tourists.

Nice interesting way to spend an hour We enjoyed a visit to this place. There were no tourist queues and to be honest a decent low number of visitors throughout. Lovely gardens and interesting to see some of the state rooms and where important parts of South Vietnam history took place. Its inexpensive and worth the money - I’d recommend and go again
The Palace was rebuilt as the original one was bombed. An imposing building, which lost its purpose once the war was over and with the transfer of power to the North

I was in this place for the first time in 1983 (I couldn't see the building), and then in 1989 I did enter it. I visited it again in November 23, and for me the decoration was not the same as now. It's not a big deal, but for those who like history it's a walk through time.

Certainly interesting. However signage as to how to view the various floors is non existent. There are 3 upper floors and 2 basement floors. The basement consists of bunkers and war rooms - both of which you don’t want to miss - but you could! The Norodom Palace is also on the grounds but signage is again poor - marked “Exhibition”. Worth spending a couple of hours here.

Unmissable piece of Vietnamese history Unmissable if you’re interested in Vietnamese history. You can see how the president lived in shameless luxury. The underground bunkers are fascinating, but are hot, crowded, and unventilated, so be prepared. No one wore a mask and I was convinced we’d catch COVID!

A great way to kill an hour or two and learn a little more about this city and country. We didn’t use the audio or a guide and instead read the notice plaques and still learnt a lot. The place is stunning to look at and be in. Beautiful architecture and a capsule of time within each room. We were there early afternoon and it was hot! Maybe an early start would be cooler? We enjoyed wandering around, and think there are lifts if you have trouble with stairs.

Nothing more than a Modern building with a good garden You read it right. Having paced up and down the independence palace (old name changed when the communists took over the south vietnam). We could only see two things worth a visit if at all. One a good picture view from the top floor of the road coming into the palace and other the war time basement war rooms. For people of Saigon this is the last historic pride left the rest having been consumed by communist suppression. For a tourist you can use your time better than spending and hour here to see 60 year old furniture in large rooms.
I understand the history & its importance but the building inside & out offers no real real spark. Its a little boring & contemporary for me.
Rebuilt after being destroyed More of a grand meeting place than a palace. The private apartments are worth seeing, but looks mo9re like a conference hall from the 70s than a palace.

Interesting…up to a point A chance to see some gruesome 1970s furniture, arranged so as to accommodate a visiting foreign delegation. And to appreciate the splendours of a brutalist concrete palace. Ok - I’m being a little facetious: This is just how the palace was before the communists seized power, and to that extent it has some interest. Perhaps most compelling is the underground bunker complete with telex centre and stone-age radio receiving equipment. The gardens are also pleasant and offer a welcome respite from Saigon’s incessant traffic. However, we found that just a little of this place went quite a long way.

Not really a ‘Palace’ Must say not a fan of 60’s concrete buildings so maybe I’m biased but didn’t think much of this one. It has historical significance to the Vietnamese but it isn’t that exciting for a foreign visitor.
Not a must see. Only interesting if you like 60's decor and architecture. Would be really boring for kids!!!

It is one of those places you can visit on hot days where you have nothing else to do. Lot of areas are restricted so can just look from outside. Still it is good place as it is near other tourist places to go.

Interesting but not exciting Someone said the basement bunker was interesting, only if you like 1970 tech equipment. The rooftop was the best in my opinion and though the rooms are well preserved it is like any other ott 60s concrete monster.

A big stark government building The place looks much more impressive from the outside than inside. The decor is very stark and you are frozen in time in 1970’s. The place is huge from the roof into the bunkers but if you’re reading the boards outside each room, you don’t get much historic information. Not the most interesting place to visit. Dinh Doc Lap - Independence Palace Hello, More information is being introduced by our tour guides. See you next time! It was very hot outside, so we decided to visit the palace. You can see several meeting rooms with carpets and armchairs inside, also the presidental bedroom and dining room and some apartments. Outside in the garden there are two tanks in front of the palace, but, and this is very important, behind, there are some bars where you can comfortably seat down and drink a smootie, aside of the very noisy streets of Saigon. We arived at 3.55pm and was told that the last ticket can only be sold at 4 pm. They obniously did not want us to visit? I asked what was there to see and unfortunatley they were not very forth coming? We said we would come back the following day. After talking to fellow toursist who had just come out of the palace , we were advised it was a waste of time! We then spoke to others that evening and they said the same . So sorry only Average for what we saw from outside. if you had been flexible then we would have visted .

Walked around the palace, not really much to see apart from some nicely decorated rooms that you aren’t allowed to enter. Roof viewing platform is ok, but it’s a lot of steps up. The bunker was also ok, just seemed like a lot of basement corridors tbh

Independence Palace or Reunification Palace. The Palace has good history but if somebody goes with an aim to see some Great Architectural beauty then one will not find anything really amazing. It is a modern kind of palace that was used by the president as control center during war. Important aspects about visiting the Palace a. Require about 1 hour to see. b. Do not miss the bunkers at the bottom of palace c. As you enter take elevator and go to the rooftop and then move down floorwise.

Interesting but could be more We spent a couple of hours looking around the Independence Palace. The building is interesting, if a little plain. The rooms a furnished mainly as meeting areas, with kitchens, radio room, etc... It could be so much more if the place was bought to life with some interactive activities or a more interesting style of presentation.
Time seems to stand still at the Independence Palace. The furniture and building design were so 60’s. Nothing very interesting. Just rooms of furniture.
The meeting rooms, dining rooms and bedrooms are interesting and, mustbe said, stunning. That said, there are better sites in HCMC for people on short visits

Adult tickets for palace and exhibition was 65 000vnd. Could spend about 4 hours max. Worth the time and money, great to wonder around and explore. Is suitable for kids but might be abit boring for them.

Interesting .... up to a point If you're visiting Ho Chi Minh City then you're going to visit The Independence Palace. It has an interesting history and some of the rooms have authentic 1960/1970's interiors. Personally I found the helicopter on the roof and telecommunications equipment in the bunker a little unnecessary but I guess it's part of the storyline.
Good building but we only stopped to see in a rush. Looked impressive but gated off. Part of a whistlestop day

Interesting but no air conditioning This place is very interesting place in both historically and visually. Grand garden and large fountain provides peaceful atmosphere, however once you enter the underground bunker facility, it gives you totally different feeling. However, all places doesn't have airconditioning at all, so if you are visiting with child, preparing water, and handheld fan will be useful.

This is a perfect example of a cold-war era building preserved for us to see. The rooms, furniture, technical equipment, cars etc are all preserved for us to see. It really does make you feel you are back in time. Worth a visit if you take an interest in these things. There is a cafe on top floor for refreshments. If you want to see all rooms/exhibits you need more than half a day.

Better if you do this with a tour guide We went with a guide to the Independence Palace as part of a Jeep tour. If we hadn’t been given additional information about each room we wouldn’t have known the significance. It wasn’t the most interesting spot but I guess it’s a significant landmark for the city so it’s kind of a must see. The grandeur of the palace is quite cool though!

You are granted entry and then on your own to walk up many flights of stairs that are super crowded with groups of school age children. Very few interp signs or explanations of what you are looking at. Only open/working restrooms are in the basement entered from outside
Nice too see, but not a must. Most of the independence Palace is sealed of from the public, and they have changed it around down in the bunker do its not original anymore.

Reccomend taking the tour and having a local guide tell you some information about what happened with the end of the Vietnam war and the history of the palace. Good opportunity for a few photos and to learn about Vietnam war/political climate of the time fromVietnam perspective

It like any other palace. If you have time time in HCM yes go visit but if short I’d skip it. We did it at the end of our day. Arrived after 4pm and stayed until it closes at 6pm. The bunker was the highlight. The surrounding area is very nice and pleasant to cool off.
Not much to see here except for the large laquer wall. I was a bit disappointed with this place. Not recommended if you are short on time.
Just a picture from afar will do As it is near the notre dame plus main area, we decided to walk and drop by but found it to be normal

Worth the visit but not my first stop. Paid for the standard entry (this includes the grounds) & the extra exhibition 65.000VND per pax. Great insight into what the Palace was like back in the war. Underground bunkers and pristine grounds were cool to see. Wish we had paid for a guide as there wasn't much information around -- however the information that was provided was also presented in English. Try and get there within enough time as we were a bit rushed. Packed with tourists but big enough for it not to be an issue.

MARGINALLY INTERESTING TO A VISITOR Now a museum recording, mainly, the last few days of the "American" war and subsequent history from the South Vietnamese perspective. Audio guides available and all notices in English and French. WCs and good disabled access including lifts. Refreshments in the grounds. TIP: Ticket office is on the far left of the building.
This is a grand palace which was used by the president of South Vietnam until reunification. Puppet regimes propped up by the US complete with Huey helicopter on the roof for a quick escape.
I accidentally visited with a friend. It's a large not air conditioned building that is sparsely decorated. However, some of the furniture was interesting.

The Independence Palace would definitely speak for itself. This walking tour is very informative as you'll get to know and learn what events transpired here that contributed to the history Vietnam. There are audio guides available for a cheap price. Make sure you bring water as this tour would revolve around walking in the entire vicinity. The entrance fee is quite cheap as well. It'd be worth your time.
A nice piece of history, especially for the Vietnamese population. Worth a short visit to take some cultural pictures.
Great to look around this iconic building, but feels laid out for the tourists, as opposed to getting a sense of how it was in it's heyday

It is a quite new building rather than being historical since it was completely rebuilt between in late 1960's. If you want to see the South Vietnam's puppet leaders' luxury life before the occupation by liberal army in 1975, you can visit.
modern and historical mixed This building is still used today for some ceremonies but most of it is about the past. Good examples of furniture of the past of the wealthy. Grounds are beautiful.
Important Retro building. Not riveting. It is an historic building and interesting to a point. A bit boring and underutilized. Maybe could be kept historic but some interest added?

It was busy with many buses visiting, but once we got in nice walk around the palace and ground floor. To be honest boring sorry to say. We did have fun in the garden looking at the tanks and the fighter plane
Nice and a must see when in hcm Visited as i think it is a must see when you are in hcm. Nice to see, it has a lot of historical history and easy to visit by yourself

History, Architecture and Furnishings This is huge mansion like structure. Each room has a design, history and furnishings. For those who crave more history about the Vietnam conflict this is a place to visit.

You will need cash to purchase ticket. Interesting open air archecture - do stop to watch the video as it gives alot of good information. We went without a guide but had a tour book to reference. Check times it is open as well because it is not open all day
Another place to visit Went here for taking some picture. We didn't buy a ticket to enter so we just take a picture outside the gate. Yes, there's a ticket counter if you would like to enter.
Looks like the picture AKA Reunification Palace it was significant during and at end of Vietnam war, apparently American war to numbers of Vietnamese. Well maintained externally.
Cool to see from afar.... But you aren't allow to go inside, so not much else to say other than you will only need to spend 5 mins to snap a shot outside the building just to say you've been there.

First time here and everyone has seen the pic of the tanks crashing through the gates in 1975. A bit boring actually and is just a big Soviet styled concrete building. The bunker was disappointing along with everything else
It doesn't have something so interesting. It has a painting though that I liked, private theater, grand dining hall and nothing else.
Is an interesting visit, mainly the bunker part You can see an special exhibition on another building if you pay an extra fee with the ticket.
Good Place for visiting The Independence Palace is big building in the city that show about government room (meeting, dinner room living, bedroom....) and show how they working with other countries.
Interesting but probably not worth the effort Not really sure of the value of this visit. We wandered around looking at state rooms and bunkers and the like but not massively interesting!

Review for Solo Travellers - Rich History with *Unsanitary* Audio Guide Hardware I visited Independence Place as a Solo Traveller. This is a huge site with tonnes to see, from the basement up to the rooftop. At the risk of duplicating content from the other 10,000 TripAdvisor reviews, I'll focus on the aspects significant to Solo Travellers like myself. This place has washrooms just down the stairs from the entrance. The whole basement area flooded when I visited, and the washrooms got closed off. This location was actually one of the best planned out and organised attractions I visited in Vietnam. The directions on which route to take, and in what order to see the rooms site were very clear and had a good logic to them. The exhibits and audio guide give you a very good sense of both the public and private lives of South Vietnamese government life during wartime. It was fascinating and informative to see everything from the bedrooms, to reception areas, to the presidential helicopter, all the way down to the wartime bunkers and war room in the basement. If you are interested in the Vietnam-USA war, you'll find the basement bunker, along with all the historical radios, communications devices, and paper maps to be engrossing. The Unsanitary Audio Guide - Oh boy, where to begin. Unlike most other places I visited in Vietnam, they actually want your passport or other ID as collateral for the Audio Guide hardware. When you're in Asia, you should always walk with an old expired passport. They never check! Furthermore, this style of Audio Guide uses those silicone earbuds, which must be pushed up into your ear canal. (See the photo attached to this review.) I wonder how many other ears these things have been inside?! Disgusting!! I squeezed a lot of hand sanitizer on those things before I used them. It would be better if they used the foam earpad type that sits on top of your ears. These audio guides typically use a standard 3.5 mm or 2.5 mm audio jack, so I'll plan to bring my own set of headphones for future trips. As for the actual Audio Guide content, this was actually the best overall audio guide I used in Vietnam. The numbered audio content, booklet and the actual physical exhibits matched up perfectly, with extremely detailed and informative content. If you want to see everything, you need half a day at least! This is definitely a must-do historical site in Ho Chi Minh City. Just remember, if you want the audio guide, bring an old expired ID and your own headphones.

We visited Independence Palace almost as an afterthought. It is preserved as a memorial and is huge and lavish in parts, but mostly somewhat austere. The rooms were a bit of a time capsule and the multicoloured phones were quirky. The basement with the various intelligence and communications systems arranged through the rabbit warren were slow to navigate as narrow and it was quite busy. The grounds were nice and the view of the boulevard from upper storied brought back memories of more dangerous times. We found it well maintained and presented with heaps of information so was interesting but unless you are a history buff or into 70's architecture / decor an hour and a half was ample for the palace and grounds.
This is were the N. Vietnamese tank broke threw the fence on that fateful day in 1973 and S. Vietnam surrendered. Nothing special, although the bunker was interesting.

For those who love history! This place is for those who are into History and keen to learn about Vietnam reunification. You can see lots of rooms that were used long time ago for important meetings. It was ok for me, not so much fun but a 45 mins well spent.
I m not a big fan of this architecture, but if you 're interested in Vient Nam's recent history, you know that Palace thai is now part of history!

Not very worthy to go inside, there were two things that I liked, the ambassador room and the bunker. There’s some history to the place and that is interesting but the majority of the rooms e very dull. I didn’t buy the ticket for the exhibits nor the audio guide.

Time capsule...dated feel A bit disappointed. Basically the palace is a dated time capsule. Most rooms are frozen in time with decor from the 50-60s. So you are walking around looking at rooms and furniture. They don’t give a lot of information about much of the history, so you may want to bone up first. Since the admission is cheap, worth a quick walk through. Just keep expectations in check.

Worth visiting but hot An interesting visit to learn about the political history of Vietnam. I found the exhibition building (separate building from the palace) to be more informative and very well air conditioned. I have to admit that I struggled walking around the palace in the humidity for too long.

60's building with interior to match I went there as I love to see the history of places I visit. An imposing building with grand rooms that showed how the other half lived while people were starving on the streets!

For me, it was just ok. If you like 1970's architecture, then this is it. It was very spartan looking. The rooms have a little note explaining what was it used for. But nothing that it made me say Wow.

From days gone by.... If you recall "Camelot' and read up on the end end of days for the Republic of Viet Nam, you'll appreciate the tour through the building. The furnishings are as they were at the time of the fall of Saigon and reminiscent of the late 60's. When you finish the tour, you can ask yourself... 'what might have been'....

Saigon place of history Welcome to history of Saigon last hundred years plus. Surrounded by very busy traffic and park of independence. In case you are interested in deeper history of Saigon the you buy ticket and go inside
You will definitely need a tour guide to understand the tour. The place is big and there may be history behind each chamber which the display board may not capture.

South Vietnam Presidential Palace This was the South Vietnamese Presidential Palace during the war between the North and South. While it is a large building and nice, it was not as elaborate as you would think considering its significance. The furnishings were fairly sparse in relation to the size of the rooms. Not one of my favorite tours.

interesting to access all areas and how the palace was used. felt like a thunderbirds set at times. projector and comms rooms look 1930s so good to see how used. the grounds are good. has greater meaning for the vietnamese as importance and impact of changes.
Visit here to have a look at the various rooms used for meetings and hosting dinners. Go with a guide to get commentary and more information, otherwise just read the boards in front of each room.

Magnificent on the outside You can give this place a miss if you don't have time in HCM. Inside the palace are rooms filled with chairs, stating who has sat there, who visited the place. Unless you are very interested in Vietnam's history, if not, you can skip this place.
Its fairly OK but not special Not very interesting and a trifle drab. The security guards are rather rude (or maybe their English is just poor).

Quiet wheelchair friendly The three Stars are only because of the people being so nice to help me through a way which was wheelchair friendly otherwise there is nothing much to do because it was like visiting a rich man's house with layout of different kind of rooms i.e. dining room, bedroom, conference room etc. Nothing that I would miss on rather I felt it was wastage of time :)
Quite an interesting place but does not offer insights of the rooms, just brief explanations. But the underground bunker is quite amazing. Just worth a quick visit
Sorry guys, nothing much to report here, the gates are lovely though. The tanks are reproductions as is the plane.
Shows you the palace as it was during the war times, but doesn't give too much historical insight. Mostly just explains what the rooms were used for.

I know it's important It just seemed as if alot of it was cordoned off. Worth going in. Poor sign postings inside but you can get onto the 2 levels of balconies facing the front lawn if you look hard.
Spent some time looking through the palace, a lot of rooms with just furniture in it, a good look back into history, only interesting part was the bunker under the palace.

I’m not really sure what we were expecting. Perhaps the term “palace” threw us off. It’s definitely large but if you’re thinking in terms of Europe or Ancient Asia, this definitely does not compare as a “palace.” Inside all you see are a bunch of large retro rooms with funky chairs. Plaques in English and Vietnamese explain what the room was used for. It’s large and there was no “path” to follow so we just wandered and assumed we saw everything, but that we cared that much because, it really wasn’t exciting. It is clean and well maintained, just not that interesting. I think maybe Vietnamese people would appreciate it more than I could. If your time in Ho Chi Minh is limited and you have a lot on your list, I would say this is a place you can miss.
a very good exhibition and a nice building Is a very nice building with nice furnitures and decoration. The most important thing is the exhibition wuth pictures and documents from vietnam war.
A must-see destination for its architectural grandeur and historic significance. There is one problem in the ticket booth. Inclusions in tour packages are very confusing.

We visited the Presidential Palace during our stay in Ho Chi Minh City, very interesting... It has a beautiful park outside. Come before 1:00 PM to get your ticket because they close after 1:00. And don't buy the souvenir bullet at the gift shop or you'll have a hard time with custom at the airport.
Unusual building very 70’s. Not worth visiting other than looking from the top floor. Or perhaps pictures from front yard.

Not an exciting tour but if ur a history buff like me then seeing the old undergroung radio room and seeing the opulence like the elephant foot unberalla stand, the old huey on the pad where the last evacuations took place, then u might be interested. I remember the iconic footage of the tanks busting down the front gates signifying the victory of the north over the south every time i ride past.

Nothing that amazing on the inside Not a massively amazing building on the inside, so my advise would be to visit from the outside and get some good photos but not really worth going inside unless you have a dire need to know about the history.
It's Ok if you are into the history of the area. Cheap 65 000 takes about 40min to walk around. Nice garden

Iconic building, famed for photos of helicopters taking off from the roof and evacuating important people to the aircraft carriers offshore at the fall of Saigon. But other that that, it is a bit boring and underwhelming. Because history is written by the victors this building and museum are not as good as they should be. Lots of old 1960s furniture and big function rooms but few photos or descriptions of events of the fall of Saigon and the South Vietnamese regime. Helicopter on the roof is nice but the tanks are not the ones which smashed down the walls and the attraction does not have any wow factor.
This was a mildly interesting place to visit, and overall we were quite underwhelmed. Some bits of interesting history, but basically just a lot of large rooms of 70's furniture and style.

Former Republic of Vietnam (South Vietnam) government building. Captured by North Vietnam in '75 and made into a museum. It was okay. The bunker was nice. Note, this place will appeal only to those who are interested in political history.

Worth a look. We went by ourselves rather than with a tour (the tours are way overpriced). You will learn a little about wartime concerns and the routines when diplomatic guests were present. There is a bunker underneath the palace that you can visit which is probably the most interesting part.
An interesting place to visit but limit your expectation. You will learn diplomatic behind the scenes details and some wartime concerns. Worth a look.
Other than the retro theme and some lovely oriental furniture, the palace itself is just ok. The gardens are also nice to see. If you have limited time, you can give it a miss.

I would say the place was nice but be careful when buy a ticket here. There are many prices and the lady who sell the ticket will give you a full package without asking you need or don't need it. A shame really!
Impressive from the outside and nice grounds but covered in no time at all, its cheap and fills time, wouldn't pay to do it again but worth a one off visit

Stop over for some photos outside It is a historic place alright. Sitting at the end of a beautiful boulevard. But nothing about the architecture outside draws our attention. So our family group voted and decided to just take some photos outside.

The Reunification Palace was set in large, park-like grounds with a fountain playing in front and a replica of the North Vietnamese tank which crashed through the front gates. Inside there were some interesting displays, some furnished rooms and a basement area but overall we found it a little bleak.

Underground bunker is pretty cool Honestly, I didn't enjoy visiting the main building that much but the underground bunker was quite amazing - it reminded me of the scenery from an old Medal of Honour game. On-site coffee shops are pretty decent.

1975, the year Bohemian Rhapsody was launched to the world and the release of the iconic photo of the tanks entering the Presidential Palace. I gave more attention to the former at the time but now glad I’ve caught up with the latter. Artefacts have been kept pretty much as they were and are well sign posted in English. Three floors of rooms and also the bunker... plus the ubiquitous helicopter on the roof.

This attraction is worth a visit. It gives a good overview of history. You can visit on your own, without a tour guide as most of the exhibits have written information that tells you about the palace. There are lifts, which I assume can be used for those with mobility issues. Note that there are three floors to view.

Interesting but the city has more to offer It’s nice to read about the history of the building, and the underground bunkers are interesting, but if you’re pushed for time then this is one you can skip.

We visited at the end of our day and were quite tired. There wasnt much information in the palace and it was difficult to know where you could enter or not. We left after 15 mins. Maybe we missed a lot but it didnt feel worth the cost. The exhibition hall alone was so much better and full of information.

Nice day out at the palace It was 8 years ago when i went there last and i saw some wonderful old maps of the Tet offensive. Also seen pictured of Lyndon Johnson and Ladybird. Regatably all has disappeared and only a few old machines are left. Still charging 40000 dong. but only a skeleton of its former glory.
We hired the audio guide to help us understand but we found it a bit dull. Beautiful building, worth a quick visit but no need to spend a lot of time here
Just okay for an hour. The most interesting part was the bunker with all the telephones and encryption machines.

Beautifully maintained We went on a half day trip with a guide. It is a lovely building but I wasn't blown away by anything much we saw. The grounds are lovely. The history of it was interesting. We didn't get to spend much time here as it was getting close to closing time, so that may have made a difference if we could have gone round it slower. It was crowded with lots of tour groups.

It was previously the Presidential Palace. There are several rooms with furniture. There is also a small garden inside. There is a helicopter on the roof of the palace and good views looking out from the balcony.

Hot, but Somewhat Interesting to a Vietnam War Buff I never did find out if we got headsets with our visit. First we were told we did, then got there and were told we didn’t get them, so we just wandered through. It’s a hot visit, but it’s interesting enough. I found the war rooms/bunker and the president’s bedroom interesting. The war rooms are not as interesting as Churchill’s but might be to a Vietnam War buff.

One of the other reviews has it right... this museum is a time capsule... it appears to be exactly the same decorations, furniture, paint... everything, that was there in 1975... it was neat seeing a building of such historical significance. It is worth a short visit for people interested in history... besides it only costs 40,000, which is less than $2, per person.
This is an average museum. The building is fine, so are the exhibitions. Still I found it a little boring. Sorry!

It's OK but not very enlighting Interesting, a massive soviet vibe building in the heart of the city. No AC available if you want to escape the heat, but shades are making the walking around easier. While you can't enter any of the rooms, you have a great view into them and you can have a look from a many different angles. There are small information boards on the walls for almost every room. The basement kitchen is the only room you can actually walk into. The helicopter is still on the rooftop and the famous Mercedes in the basement. Interesting, but not very enlightening piece of history.

Visit if you are a history buff Nothing extraordinary,if you are not interested in USA- Vietnam war. However the palace gardens have some beautiful bonsai trees and is a great place to relax in the afternoon sun.

A visit will show you have the leaders in Hanoi survived during the US Viet Nam war. You can see the bunker in the basement that would protect the north leaders from all bombs. You can see the communications center as well as the list of troop deployment at that time listing more than 450,000 US troops.
Probably me not doing my homework properly but I didn't find this palace as interesting as I thought. There's plenty of rooms to visit but nothing that made me say WOW .

The Independence Palace in District 1... beautiful place. Entry ticket price also cheap. Good environment. Big garden. Nice take pictures. Only one problem. Toilets not clean in the palace. Bad smell. Tourist attraction is good... must improve.

Interesting to see for once Interesting to see for a once and walk around all those rooms and to see official rooms and private rooms of president and his vice president as well . it was nice to visit the roof also with view to helicopter place and marked area from bombs

Quite a story to tell ... It was pretty packed when we visited - so it was often difficult to see and read the explanations - but nevertheless this building holds many memories of some very important times and some equally important decisions.

The Vietnamese are proud of this building; westerners may be less impressed although there are some attractive features inside and out, but nothing to get too excited about. (The bunker may be of interest to radio buffs).

Was kinda disappointed by my visit to the Independence palace. Its more of display of rooms, furniture, sofas, dining tables and mind you , I am not kidding you. Less of History, Better off visiting the War Remnants Museum instead.

First, understand that the grounds close at noon for an hour. we were part way through touring the building when doors were closed and we were directed to leave. If you want to see the whole place, give yourself a couple of hours.

The building is a collection of well appointed rooms that you can view from the cordoned off doors only. There are many state and conference rooms, a large banquet room, the personal quarters, a balcony that has a terrific view of the gardens and the fountains. The basement reminded me of movies on the world war 2 era (the steel desks, telephones, mobile radios, telex machines etc) There is a lot of history to this building and a good guide can bring the building to life. In my opinion this attraction can be skipped if you are short on time.

The major attraction for me was the tank that crashed through the gate on the last days of the war still remain on the palace ground. A little offended by the description given by the guide on the tour.

Worth a visit but not a long stay For only $1.50 entrance this is worth a visit to wander the grounds and explore the rooms and basement of the palace. There are some interesting snippets of information but mostly they just provide context on meeting rooms (which we didn’t find hugely interesting). However, a good follow on from the war museum nearby.

Worth seeing if you’re at a loose end This is often listed as one of the ‘must-see’ places in Ho Chi Minh city but, to be honest, you won’t miss a huge amount if you don’t get to it. It’s interesting as far as its role in history is concerned - home to the government in the south that opposed the Communist movement in the north and the ‘liberation’ of the south was signaled by a tank bursting through the front gates. Other than that, it’s particularly interesting for its architecture and the way internal decorations represent styles of the time. Look out for the sickly yellow presidential bathroom!

Visit to the old Independence Palace Interesting visit through history - the building has seen better days but shows how things used to be back in the 70's when this was in use Worth a visit just to remember old Saigon

Interesting history, but wasn’t open We walked past the front of the Independence Palace on a recent visit to HCM. We would have liked to have gone inside, however it was closed over the lunchtime period.

The vast proportion of the palace is a qusi museum which purports to show the building and its fittings almost as it was left when it was abandoned by the previous South Vietnamese Administration. It is not an attractive building in itself- a typical 1960s monolith - and one could be forgiven for questioning what it is all about....

Close your eyes and imagine this is some time from 1965-1975. The building and decor look a bit like a time capsule from that era. This was South Vietnam's version of the White House, now preserved as a museum. There is not much to occupy a long visit, but it is worth a quick visit. It captures some of the brief history of the leadership of South Vietnam before the fall of Saigon.
