Lovely place and I understand how important it may be for vietnamese but though it was a bit boring. The sorroundings are perfect, place is huge and perfectly kept just not much actual value. I would probably recommend reading online about it to see if it fits your desires.

Not a great deal of information on this otherwise huge, somewhat sterile edifice. Worth a visit if you've got an hour or so but if you want to get a real feel for what life used to be like, there are other places to go that are better value for money.

Historical information It is a good place to visit to get a feeling of how the country was during the war. But I think they didn't have too much information and it would be nice to go on the guided tour, if you wanna spend the money.

lovely grounds but underwhelming Worth the small fee to walk around the grounds and see the palace but it's really just a tour of rooms and not a history lesson. Absolutely skip the extra $ for the audio. I walked through in 15 minutes and was done. Worth it if you are nearby, but for me, not worth the cab to and from our hotel.

A fun experience but slightly underwhelming We as a group had a interesting experience. The exhibit at the start was amazing with its interactive learning with tv screens explaining Vietnam life pre war. As the exhibit finished explaining the war and the history of the Independence Palace we moved onto the Place itself which required extra tickets. The Palace was not as lavish as the outside and most places are closed off. The information was not as elaborate and some information was repeated. The children thoroughly enjoyed the exhibit but them too agreed they expected more of the Palace and got a tad bored throughout. Overall the Palace exhibit is worth a definite look but try not too expect too much for the Palace itself.

Really nothing much to see. It’s history can be read. There’s not much opulence or anything as such. So it can be missed if you’re pressed for time. Most of interest was the underground offices where you can envision how decisions are made and meetings held.

This is where South Vietnamese leaders managed to lose the war. A couple of coups, a few state dinners, lots of mid century furniture, the war room a few photos make for an interesting discussion. The gate that the NVA tank broke through has been repaired but the tank or replica sits near by.

Nice palace with not too much to see It’s a nice garden and a nice palace but there aren’t too many things to see in the palace itself. Generally it’s not as big and luxurious as palaces in other countries around the world (this is probably the case in some other palaces and temples in Vietnam). A few advices: buy to full package to access both buildings and museums as the second one has a lot of good information and photos And most importantly: plan ahead properly as this place is closed at lunch time and opens after linch from 2pm

Historic, I suppose, but the information is more interesting than the building itself. An obligatory stop when in Ho Chi Minh City. We thought it interesting, but the tour was a real yawn. We could have skipped fighting the massive crowds just to catch a glimpse of not very personal or interesting over decorated rooms. We'd have enjoyed a nice lecture on the building a whole lot more.

Not much here other than period furniture Didn't get much out of this one to be honest. It was a building filled with period furniture from the 60s that looked untouched since. But really that was all.....didnt find it overly interesting. The bunkers in the basement were a little bit more interesting, but overall if we had skipped it I wouldn't have been disappointed. Price was right though at about $2 entry.
It is only open before 11h00. Just a large building with many rooms. Well preserved. The bunker is interesting.

My friend and I enjoyed the walk through. the biggest dressing room/closets ever seen. Sort of a 60's motif frozen in time, a bit large and stark for anyone to actually live in, but I guess they did. Don't miss the lower level with some of the bunker areas accessible. Not well mapped out or marked, so we missed the rooftop where we saw some people up there as we exited the premises.
After the war remnants museum this was not as interesting. Lots of state rooms to look at behind a roped barrier. Nice to see but didn’t need to spend long there.

Beautiful building within a large park. Only observed from outside the gates. Easy walk from the markets. Historical significance from the famous picture of the North Vietnamese tank rolling over the front gate.
Just saw from outside The palace was not open for visit as we went there around 11am. From outside it seemed gorgeous but be careful with tour timing if you want to see inside as well.

It’s very 1960’s and not really a palace, It’s a cold building without any real history only that the presidents had meetings there during and after the war. You have to go but just take a photo of the outside as you won’t be missing anything inside!

The only remarkable thing about the building in my opinion is its history. The famous film of the ganks crashing through the gates leading to the end of the Vietnam War. The building is well designed but not exceptional, the furniture as one would expect for a high ranking building, but it is neither over or underwhelming.

Very interesting. Make sure to make your way to the top floor if you are interested to see the President's "escape" Huey. Good photo op where the NVA Armour smashed through the front gate, symbolically signalling the fall of Saigon.

Worth a visit takes a little while to go through but the history and the rooms won’t disappoint simply amazing and provides a little bit of a story through out. Easy to navigate through yourself. Audio is available to hire if you want the full experience solo.

Major tourist site in HCMC. Located in downtown area. Not much inside the building. Just to walk around the grounds and inside the building. Can see things like president sleeping quarters and under the building to walk around the old bunker. Outside in the top right corner of the grounds is another building holding a special exhibit of the development of the building complex. VND65 for entry into palace grounds and palace building, as well as entry into the special exhibit. Can buy the ticket to just enter the palace grounds and palace building which is slightly cheaper

History - yes; but not great Since we have been to places like Churchill War rooms in London and Sheikh Mujibur Rehman's house in Dhaka, this building seemed a little less historically or politically rich. The three upper floors which have various rooms, helipad, cinema hall, etc can be quickly browsed through. The underground chambers consisting of the bunker, communication equipment, etc evoke a little more interest. Overall, the Reunification building is at maximum a one time pass through.
Enjoyed architecture and the old Soviet tank and airplane. Interesting to hear history. Fortunately our guide gave concise interpretation of history.
Interesting displays of an important facility but not a must-see. Some cool furnishings, interior designs and architecture. Nice grounds.

So this is a huge Palace, takes 3 hrs to cover, if you want to take your time. Lots of rooms, Movie Hall etc. I somehow was not blown away by it. But I must say, it was very well maintained & managed.

Best part was the bunker Best part of the whole palace was the bunker under the palace itself. Gets really busy so hard to read all the information. Was interesting enough but found the museum better.
It was so so, i think it overated . I like war remnant museum more and the post office was better. If you have less time you can skip this

Great history and insight to the formation of modern Vietnam We went there on a Friday. It's not the easiest place to find your way around. There were lots of closed off areas and, rather bizarrely, by about 1115-1130 we had staff (who might have been soldiers from their uniforms) either telling us that some parts were shut and/or moving us on purposefully around the 'route' ! Perhaps they shut for lunch but we were not warned about that on entering - and they had good signs, information etc in English. Anyway, it's interesting and well worth a visit.
Worth seeing but not a high light Interesting enough to see the various rooms that were left exactly as they were in the 70's. Worth a look but not a highlight of your time in Vietnam
We simply bought entrance tickets and walked through the building. To be honest there wasn't much exciting to see. Next time I would probably get a guided tour.

Indeed a palatial building that speaks to the South Vietnamese government's opulence during the war years. Well worth an hour visit to appreciate the times as most of the rooms are left in the 60's decorating style. Interesting feature on the roof where the bomb was dropped - a fragment signed by the pilot sits on the roof.
Remnant of Vietnam war A palace where the President operated the war room during Vietnam war. It looks average Govt building. The bunker is tough and long. Nothing worth seeing.

If you have a lot free time, and don’t what to do then u can check this place. I had friend visiting me in Saigon, so I decide to show him local sights, and check for my self too. So I found this place little bit boring, you can see old furniture, rooms of government people, read some information about past politic people life. Huge place, have to walk a lot, and weather was very hot...

If you have the time... Ho Chi Minh City is not like Bangkok or Tokyo. There isn't that much to see. If you've committed yourself to a day of local sightseeing, then the Independence Palace with be on your itinerary. Other TripAdvisor reviewers have done a good job of describing this location. I liked: The two Chinese tanks outside and the pictures of Nixon inside (and trying to figure out where he sat). I didn't like: The confusing set of arrows for self-guided tours.

I can't say that I enjoyed this place very much I did a lot of reading but all you can really see is a lot of furniture and not much else there's lots of tables lots chairs lots of lounges one big helicopter out the back after about the third room it became boring

Interesting half day tour My sister in particular enjoyed the tour of the palace. I personally would have liked to have seen more detail and information about individuals. The audio commentary devices provided were very good and from a personal point of view I thought there was plenty of seating provided for those tourists like myself who like to periodically sit down
Interesting piece of history We had a guide and spent just over a hour here not sure if you can do it without a guide. Get to see most of the building

Other than a little more insight on the well coveted war and some fairly modest history about previous use it’s now nothing more than a modern (ish) hotel looking building that gets rented out by big corporations as a fancy / historical venue to host corporate events at.
Not that interesting for me, good place for a quick stop. Big museum where you get to see some recent Vietnam history. The downside is that some explanations are not translated into English.

Palace Has No Air-conditioning Inside The palace is beautiful only outside as it's massive building but when u walk a tour inside there is no air conditioning and u will sweat like standing in Tandoor Oven . The tour of this place available in English . French and Vietnamese Language .

The,palace has no air conditioning and is hot to walk around. Lots of stairs. The grounds are beautiful and had a nice breeze. Pretty sure you have to pay entry to access the park. I found the documentary the most interesting and the bunker was ok. Possibly would mean more to the Vietnamese people.

Classic place to visit in Ho Chi Min but not really interesting if you don't mind too much about Vietnam War. I enjoyed it but I personally skipped some part of my audio guide (MORE EXPENSIVE THAN TICKET) since I preferred to save time for more waking in the city. If you like historical facts about Vietnam War it's absolutely a must to visit.

Probably worth a look once the works are completed. Doesnt stand out in its current state. However, it is across the poad from the post office, so easy to include. Once works are complete it should be beautiful

some history highlights Some state rooms and some history of the palace under the South Vietnamese government available. A must do for locals. Nice gardens. Entry fee. Closes over lunch. Not the most fascinating thing about HCMC.

just a building which was for president in French time. not much things to visit. if you have not much time, suggest you can skip this spot sincerely. 只是一個法國時期給當時總督的建築，類似總統府，沒什麼東西可看，如果你沒什麼時間的話建議可以跳過這個景點。

It was a palace where the president lived i guess. May be of historical significance which am not much aware. But personally felt, a pic standing outside will make check box tick in your bucket list than a walk through all the rooms (just a personal opinion). Kids will get bored.

Came here looking for a respite from HCMC's sweltering heat and were disappointed to find no air conditioning. It's interesting to look around the rooms, but the building itself is plain and simple. Takes a while to find the entrance.

The only thing worth seeing in the Big Ho Chi Minh - Siagon to us VN vets This was the US Embassy and home to several battles on the Chinese New Year eve's of 68 and 69. Also home to many Vietnam Presidents for over 100 yrs. All were killed while in office. Rumor has it ...that the large fountain in front is bad Karma...never put a fountain in front of your home. Very nice inside and has the eloquence of a Presidential palace. Takes about 2-hrs to see all the features. Again, this is the best Saigon has to offer. See it and then head to the beach. Shopping is not that good mostly Chinese junk repackaged. Nothing worth buying and a waste of time. The people are very friendly and all the kids want to talk to you in English.

Easy to find and only 40k to get in. But I’m still not sure what we were supposed to be impressed by. There’s a lot of history that’s happened here but the cavernous cabinet and meeting rooms look like something out of North Korea. Just. Plain. Ugly. The whole place feels like the worst hotel & conference centre on Earth. We headed for the exit 15 minutes later. Go if you must.

It’s an empty building There wasn’t much to this? It’s a massage empty palace that seems wasteful really. Some beautiful furniture and paintings but that’s about it? There’s no air conditioning so it gets very hot! C Could have happily missed this tour

Well, we were kinda disappointed with the palace. I mean, it's not bad but nothing special. You can see some conference rooms, offices, and other historical important rooms there. But that is not much different from the European government buildings.
Worth seeing from the outside. We didn't have time to go in, and suggest that of you wish to do so, you should go early.

Beware of the coconut man This happened to us and another family that we later met so we just wanted to warn people. We went to go to the palace, walking there from our hotel and was stopped by a man selling coconuts. At first he started telling my sons that they looked strong and that they could carry his coconuts as they were heavy - he was happy and smiling and we thought it was amusing and he was being friendly so my two sons posed for photos holding the long stick with coconuts in barrels on both ends. No sooner had we done this, then the man had cut the tops off 3 coconuts for us, which we felt we could not say no to. He charged us 200,000 vnd EACH for them. I told him it was too much and he reduced it to 350,000 vnd for all three of them. This was our second day so we weren't quite used to the money but Coconuts are usually about 35,000vnd each in cafes etc so we were well and truly ripped off. A day later we met another family on a tour who told us the exact same story. Same guy, same approach to them and their sons, same area around the palace. Beware everyone. I wish we had been warned.

Well preserved Time Capsule of a palace The palace is pretty decently preserved considering it hasn't been actively used for quite a while. It was cool to walk through and see the various rooms, and the underground bunker. The dance floor at the top was neat to see but very crowded. It is a good stop to make from a historical perspective. If you are really into architecture and the history of the area this will probably be a great place to stop. For us it was neat to see but did not have any kind of wow factor beyond it simply being a large building.

Without wanting to take any political part in Vietnam history, we found the luxury of this place disgusting as it demonstrates the luxurious decision-taking existence of a corrupted system... The building itself is nothing very special - it only got some interest as it was the location where decisions were taken which lead to unnumberable sufferings of the vietnamese civil population.

We initially thought that the place was maybe telling something about the reunification of the north and the south but it was basicly just the building with some facts about the time when the last president was living there.

The palace is a to visit place in HCMC as it was a fundamental pinpoint in the history of Vietnam. Inside the rooms are beautiful but outher than a nice walk it is not that interesting. What maybe worth more a visit is the bunker and the nice and quite garden with bars to take a break from the chaos of the city

The palace only offers views of rooms that were used not so long ago after the American war, so if you were looking for a more exciting/ adventurous thing-to-do in Ho Chi Minh and learn more about the war, you’re better of going to the war remnants museum and just see this building from the outside.
Just plain rooms with nothing in them, kids didn't rate this attraction at all . We only spent about 1.5 hrs in it and could have been less

Good to go through but a bit boring It’s a great looking building and some good history there but was a bit boring to go through and read somethings, more of a been there now when we see a documentary

A city tour of Ho Chi Minh... It was nice to see Independence Palace with the manicured lawn and stately rooms. However, it was just that with no “ooomph” if you know what I mean. If allowed, I’ll save my 40 VND and just take photos from the outside. It’s not much but it will save you time.

Only from the outside This beautiful building can only be viewed from the street. It is situated on approximately 25 acres, with lovely parkland. The original gates that were rammed during the war and still in situ.

Not very much impressive when we Visited this Museum i was not very much impressed and i had this feeling even before i go there, i just think that if you visit saigon it is worth to visit since it is a part of History. a place which you can know more about the country. Mostly meaning behind the Building than fascination.

The Independence Palace is definitely worth a look, just for its place in history. It is a brilliant looking building and inside is interesting. If you are doing the War Remnants Museum, then it is well worth popping in here too.
This visit to the Palace was worthwhile visit as the building has been maintained showing a snapshot of history.

I think TripAdvisor is not to be trusted so I guess don't trust my review? Lol Honestly the Palace isn't as great as people make it out to be. Audio or guide totally not necessary and honestly 1.5 hrs is plenty of time. The bunker and secret entrance for the president was kinda cool but the rest us just old furniture without real character. It's like, "the president sat there" and "oh that's where the president would have parties" and it just doesn't come to life. I felt bored out of my mind and I had high expectations after the War Museum but unfortunately it's average at best. If you're bored there then go otherwise you won't miss much
We visited in the morning and there weren’t as many people there as when we were leaving. I don’t think the audio tour is necessary as everything is well marked and numbered. Good place to visit.
Concrete for communism. We took a photo at night, but didn’t go in - if we could? Colonial part of the town and worth passing by.

Spent about 1.5 hours here. I was bored. Honestly, the audio guide and the signs tell you a bit about the place but it’s just so specific to a certain part of the war and the building itself is not that nice so overall it’s very underwhelming. If you’re bored or a huge Vietnam war buff, sure, otherwise, don’t plan your day around it.
Big and Grand Palace. Beautiful light. Nice furniture. Gives you a bit of insight into Vietnamese Politics. Helicopter on top was nice. Quite busy though. Lots of stairs
This historical place was one of the tourist attractions we visited in Ho Chi Minh City. Nothing really special except the story which yoy can read on the internet.
Normally I don't really like these type of buildings but the interiour, architecture and history grabbed our attention. Some extra information about the place would have been nice.
The palace has been demolsihed and rebuilt after the bombing incident. The new palace carries the 70's design and architecture, felt boring and not interesting. I wouldn't recommend.
Well kept museum with heaps of historic relevance. The exhibits are really well maintained and explanations are in English, French and Vietnamese. Worth a visit.

We enjoyed walking around and finding out information and enjoyed looking at the communications bunker and former president war room etc etc but not much else. Grounds are lovely and cafe on site too. War Remanents museum is said to be better value but we didn't see that ourselves.

This is a chance to look at lots of rooms. It makes more relevance if you go to Hanoi and see how Ho Chi Minh lived. He did not live in this type of luxury at all. The most interesting part for me was the architecture and how it was designed to keep cool by use of the stones at the front and the back of the building.

The grounds are nice but inside is a bunch of rooms that were used to host dignitaries. Only visit if you have extra time but not a necessity. Cost is around 60k VD(40to the grounds and 25 to go inside).

Go early to avoid the crowds As with most sites in HCM it is always best to go early and avoid the big crowds. Lots more information stations have been established since my first visit which makes it a lot more interesting.

Nice building mainly showing the previously used government office's and rooms, artworks and furnishings. A helicopter on the roof and tanks, jet plane on the grounds. A nice park area to walk around and get out of the sun.
Important historically, but there is very little to see and learn from this place. There are much more interesting places to visit in HCMC, such as the War Remnants Museum.

A pretty palace from the South Vietnamese era. The decor is from the 70’s, but gives a glimpse into the inner workings of the palace at its high point. Guests can walk throughout- from receiving rooms to private quarters.

Interesing but too much of the same thing. Whilst it is interesting to see some of the rooms it is just more of the same, other than different décor or colours. If you are into this type of thing then you should go, and there is definately a lot of history behind it. Whilst I found some of this interesting, I would not suggest it is a place that you must visit, but it doesn't cost much to get in and it is in a really lovely park that you should also spnd some time walking through. If you have some free time it is worth a visit.

There isn't a lot to see here. I'm a bit of a history buff so wanted to take a look, but it's a building with a bunch of empty rooms you walk around and observe. Try the audio tour if you're interested, however you won't need to spend more than 90 minutes here. Children would be bored.

All you will see are the rooms where history took place, but it’s still kind of cool to be there. I think it’s worth the time. It can be done in less then 90 minutes. It’s kind of sparse, but a nice building and kind of calm. No A/C bring a fan. Much better than the HCM museum surprisingly.

Only worth a trip if you have historical interest The building is not well maintained. There is little or no air conditioning. Although there is great local historical significance, it is not worth visiting unless you want to take a trip back to the 70’s. Furnishings were not particularly ornate or exciting when originally installed, 50 years later they just look for it years old. I could have skipped this visit.
a part the park, the visit is boring Great 60's french architecture, fantastic park, for the rest is boring - avoid to go when is raining :)
Too large to spend hours As a part of the big group, we visited the palace which gave the history of the war time and a bit tiring in short time when we had to see many places

Independence Palace - We spent about 2 hours there with a guide. The guide was good, the palace was a bit boring. The top levels had the carious meeting rooms where the presidents received their guests and the bedrooms/dining rooms of the Presidents - Fairly lavish, but we only saw them cause we were there! The basement was more interesting with the old-time communication rooms, and bunkers. Shoes: Comfortable shoes recommended. No restriction on water etc, so feel free to carry your own water bottles .

Interesting time capsule of the 1960's/early 70's This palace was an OK visit and you feel like you're in a 60's/70's time capsule. The grounds are beautifully looked after and a nice stroll around. My favorite spots here was up on the roof and in the war rooms in the basement. If you are limited for time, you could skip this in favor of the War Remnants museum which is much more interesting.

A very informative place or old palace if you really want to know about Vietnam Presidents and their VIP visitors to this palace. This is where Vietnam was liberated after a tank gate crashed into this palace.
Ok to visit if u have timw The palace is filled with rooms of big halls where the VIP holds their meetings and a big garden with a coffeehouse. And don’t forget visit the bunker.

The lawn of the palace is what will surely take your breath away. From the balcony on first floor you can look out on the road as the traffic keeps moving. The place is well kept and proper discriptions are provided at every place of interest

I didn't find it very impressive. That being said, we didn't do a (audio) tour and we're not that much interested in politics/architecture from the 1970's. It's also really big and a lot of walking (and stairs). So if you don't have much time, don't waist your time in here. Better visit the War Remnants Museum.
We visited the Palace as part of our city tour and had a guided tour explaining the different rooms and history, good for kids. An hour looking around is enough for families with kids.
Ok, if you have the time, then visit if not, you're not missing out on anything. It reminded me of an airport terminal in a dictatorship African country of yesterday....

The Reunification Palace The Reunification Palace, as it is known today, was the home & workplace for the President of South Vietnam during the Vietnam War & the location where the said war came to an end when a North Vietnamese tank crashed through its gate. After entering the gates to the palace, you will pass by a well manicured lawn with a large fountain. Amongst the rooms we visited were the cabinet room, conference room, national security chamber & the presidential office. All of them appeared to be preserved in the dated era of time. There are placards with era pictures depicting the rooms you are viewing. On the top of the building is a helipad with helicopter. A nice bit of history to complement your visit of the War Remnants Museum. I would rate this attraction above average, GOOD.
Place to stop for a photo of this impressive building whilst wandering around or on way to war remnant museum,which is nearby.

We went and had a look. They have North Vietnamese Army tanks pointing at the palace and it looks like the old North is still flexing its muscles and reminding the south that they lost the war of freedom and the North has control.
We went last 13th of May 2018. The entrance fee was 40,000 dong. The museum doesn’t have air conditioning. Extremely hot on summer. Most of the rooms have barriers. We took photos outside each room.

The visit to the Independence Palace was not impressive. The architecture was from the 1970's and so was the interior decoration. Not much to see there, only a couple conference rooms and the bedrooms... Wouldn't waste my time again

I mean... it's a big house Wasn't sure what I was expecting walking into a palace. But hot damn was I underwhelmed. Rooms were roped off and people in the tour would rush to take photos of the room full of old school furniture from the doorway. Architecture of the windows on the second floor was cool enough for some nice photos if it's not too busy. So at least there's that.
nothing much to see here, its like an old house many tourists here, chopper at rooftop, tanks in the garden and a jet fighter too, big garden and see the old ancient rooms of vietnam leaders

Beautiful building and grounds from the outside. Building has been kept for use as museum with rooms left how they used to be. We had a tour guide with us which helped. Without this you’re looking at a bunch of rooms which mean nothing. You get to see meeting/planning rooms along with all the communication rooms down in the bunkers.

Interesting but only visit if you have lots of time to spare Cheap to get in, lovely gardens which also have some old tanks and airplanes to see. Lots of rooms to look at, each with a description outside the door. Wouldn't recommend for young children as not very interactive.
Enjoyed the visit here. Informative and interesting, although a lot of that was the enthusiasm of our guide.

Historical place, but would not return There’s a free shuttle ride that takes you around the former president’s house. This is part of your admission. There is only one entrance so google map it before heading there. We walked around the entire block which took approx 20-30 mins before finding the entrance.

Might have been better with the audio tour As a fully government run site, the staff are disinterested and unhelpful. The building itself is an OTT palace created around 1954 by the dodgy South Vietnamese government to replace an equally OTT French building on the same site, and when the communists won the war in 1975, they've kept as a monument to the glorious state. Make sure you visit the underground lair/bunker (no doubt similar to those most despot leaders have) although we got the distinct impression that most of the genuine fittings down there have long since gone.
A Reminder Of a Troubled Past This attraction was part of our tour in Ho Chi Minh city . There are many other more worthwhile sites to be seen but if you have the time can be educational

Interesting piece of history I would recommend getting audio when visiting for a more detailed explanation of the palace. Interesting but if you are pressed for time then give it a miss. Well managed but not well ventilated.

I visited the Palace with a friend of mine while i was on a recent business trip to Vietnam. She rented the headset for me with audio in English and she had a map (i don’t think the one she had was in English) but we had a lot of trouble matching up the audio with the room descriptions because there are no numbers or signs outside the individual rooms or displayed items to let you know which button to push on the audio. We got confused more than once. The audio has TONS of information for each stop in the Palace, so much so that I didn’t listen to most of it. And I’m a big history buff. All in all, it’s worth it because it tells a story about a time not so long ago of the American War (the Vietnam War to we Americans). There is evidence of the storming of the Palace by the Viet Cong, tanks out front on display, and a war era helicopter on a (third ?) floor roof that you can clearly see from one of the windows on that floor. It’s a beautiful Palace and so full of information that you could probably spend a day there and still not get it all.
Formerly used by the South Vietnamese government before unification this is now just a monument to history and a bit bland
If you are in the area and wish to check off a tourist attraction, it's worth a visit. I did not find it particularly interesting.
series of reception rooms set up asper 1960's. bunker underneath was interesting.. inportant to the vietnamese people.

Unless you are in to history a visit here can get a little boring after a while! We had a your through the palace and or guide explained the various rooms etc. What was interesting was the underground rooms. There are communications rooms complete with the original equipment, a kitchen and we also saw a room full of old typewriters. If you have time visit our else skip this and you won't miss anything!
Interesting architectural design. Good to hear of the recent history in what appears to be an impartial way. Enjoyed seeing the bunker area.

Took a look from outside I was running out of time and took a look from outside. The place was vast and behind the reunification palace it is a open picnic area for locals where foreigners and tourists are not allowed. I entered there by mistake and was driven away :-) and asked to go to the front gate which was a half a mile walk around the walls of the palace. Since i lost time finding the right entrance i lost time and had to satisfy myself with a outside peak only :-) attaching the photo of the walk around and the front picture.

It's an iconic building and worth a view. It loses it appeal in how lots and lots people were shuffled through a side stairwell. The stairs were packed since there is only one elevator and no A/C in the whole place so it was stifling hot. They let you go to the roof and the view is really nice. They could use some fans or room A/Cs to help since it's a popular place for foreigners and Vietnamese school groups.
What an odd visit. My husband hated it, but there were bits that I found interesting but there are better places to visit if time is short as ours was.

It was now my second time there and they changed the room with war photography into a souvenir shop... in this palace you can see how the president lived and there are just many rooms with chairs.. the problem is that there are also many people who smells like they forgot to clean up after they used their after. But all in all it's okay to see it once when you are interested in the Vietnamese history and sweeting and after smell.

Well laid out, but no real atmosphere The building and its history is of interest but there is no real atmosphere to it. It would have been nice to also see some history related to the previous building and events in the building since the end of the war.

It was an interesting place highlighting the political events leading up to the VC victory over Saigon in 1975. You get to see the rooms as they were from the 1960s to 1970s, and learn about the key meetings that were conducted in them. The palace actually had a history dating back to the 1800s, but you don't learn about much of it before the 1960s. There are underground bunkers which you might miss if you don't find the correct staircase. It closes for lunch so be sure to avoid lunchtime. There's no aircon either so choose your timing right. We went in right after it opened at 1pm and came out dripping in sweat.

Can be seen if you have time I have seen the war remnants museum and Cu Ci tunnels before and hence found reunification palace to be okay. However, its a different experience and shouldn’t be compared. I did it because I had a lot of time in Saigon. Keep it at the bottom of the list if you have a tight schedule.

Historic but not that interesting Our guide certainly spoke well to the significance of the Palace to his country's history. Throughout the building there are placards that explain the importance of the various rooms and the historical context for some of them. The rooms are certainly well maintained, some dating back to the 1950s. All that said, I found the most interesting part of the building to be the bunker in the basement. Not to take away the importance of the palace for the Vietnamese, I have to say there wasn't all that much here to hold my attention.
It is a complex, with large rooms, but each one does not have that much to see. If you are short on time, I would not bother going.
It is a building you need to see if you want to give your visit to Vietnam some historical justification. Interesting, but not really a historical building that makes you excited.

A ghost shell of the past. It seems most of the history and artefacts have been removed leaving the building itself seemingly hollow. The Presidential reception rooms are quite ornate especially the Ambassador reception room with its magnificent lacquered, multi-panelled backdrop. The fortified basement is quite stark, devoid of many features depicting what was once the central command point for the South Vietnamese government. Worth a visit just to stand in the spaces and in the grounds where many important events occurred during the Vietnam War.

Interesting but don’t spend too long there I came here with 2 friends and it was worth the visit but only because we were in the area, not too much information there so would recommend picking up the guide at the start.

It’s basically , an office building . You can tour conference room , meeting rooms and some more conference rooms. If you have an office block nearby , then you can replicate the experience without living home .

This wasn't for us. I realise it's very important as a symbol of independence for the country but it was a huge building, impressive sized rooms etc and zero atmosphere or sense of anything. If you have plenty of spare time then give it a whirl. I wish our guide had spent more time at the museum and omitted this.
Nothing special about this Nothing much to see in this place. I felt that once I've seen one room, the next looks quite the same. Didn't even get to see the entire building as we got quite bored.

A rather huge palace with lots of things to see. Considering Vietnam is all about history, this is must visit place if you are into Vietnamese history. Plenty of guided tours available in multiple languages. Tickets are super cheap around 1.25 pounds Sterling per person.

This building and its furnishings is preserved almost as it was in 1966. That being said, it is pretty unexciting. The furnishings and pictures in the Ambassador’s Chamber with its Japanese style lacquer work was the highlight for us.
ok if you like to look at old houses and its decor. but its not really my cup of tea , but my partner found it interesting .
The Independence Palace The Independence Palace is interesting to see. No one lives here anymore; it is used for state visits.
Lovely building, worth a visit if you’re in area. Didn’t actually excite me, think I had seen so much more beauty in HCM City
Very well spreaded. Open space with the foundations all around. Nothing more than that. It's more for local people for rallys, gathering or for recreation activities.

Surprised it wasn’t ransacked It was quite interesting to see a moment in time (with the palace frozen from its 1975 decor) in the context of a day when we’ve also seen the Cu Chi tunnels & war museum but it’s not a great standalone trip. Was a bit like visiting the set of Tracey Island
We didn’t take tour guide because we can borrow the radio translator at the reception and it was enough to understand about the place!
Interesting view of the hierarchical past in Vietnam. Multiple storeys with limited lift access to the upper floors.

The reunification palace Worth a visit as it has been kept in the same state as it was the day the Americans left. The tanks in the grounds however are copies. The iconic original that crashed the gates is in Hanoi.

Can't really say this was an exciting place to visit. It's just part of Saigon's history - something you saw on your tv during The Vietnam war. Glad I've seen it but I wouldn't return as it's quite boring.
The bunker is definitely worth seeing. Especially the communication room and the president bedroom underground.

Being a bit of a history buff have seen the famous clip of the tanks driving through the front gates, it was good to go there and soak up the atmosphere. Palace it self a series of different rooms with furniture, we were lucky to have a guide to explain what was what though they blurred after the 3rd one. Down stairs in the bunker a bit more interesting where the South Vietnamese ran there part of the war from. Probably more good then average and worth a visit.

Apart from the replica USSR and Chinese tanks which crashed through the Palace gates in 1975 to mark the official end of the Vietnamese civil war, and the victory of the North, the remainder of the venue is typically dull Soviet architecture. Not recommended unless you are a true history fanatic.
We were on a tour so our guide gave us a lot of information about the building. I think one of the most interesting areas was the basement with bunkers and lots of war history.

Really hot there! I didn't so my research about the palace, I would do it when I was in there and I thought it would be a little about the war but it was only about who held meetings in one room and another. Really boring for me. But probably interesting for others.

Don’t bother with the audio guide Spent a couple of hours visiting the Independence Palace. The audio guide is a waste of time as there is only the same limited information as provided on wall plates beside each room. Interesting architecture and a lovely quiet space on the top floors. Some of the artworks are very special. The historical significance of what took place here make it a worth while visit. Loved the flowering jasmine trees.

Nothing too interesting This is a basic building with little furniture inside but gives some insight into the past. Not very interesting or exciting but worth popping in just to know what’s there. We went as part of the city tour

quite a boring place, but nice grounds The best things about the palace is the grounds and the outside of the building. Beautiful fountains, and impressive building and pretty green spaces around. The view from the balcony and the helipad looking out to the city is great. Inside it's not so good. There are a few rooms that are interesting, whether that's because of the history or what is in there. However, too many of the rooms simply have no explanation at all, some of them even don't have anything in there! Compared to some of the other buildings and museums in HCMC the palace was disappointing.

Somewhat modern and stark in appearance. This was built by Diem who we supported a coup against in early 60's. I guess modeled after the White House? The admission is almost 3 times the cost of the remnants museum and it is not nearly as interesting.

Impressive tourist site IF you can stomach Ho Chi Minh City/Saigon this is a must-see. (I couldn't: the air pollution was even worse than Beijing and I didn't have a quiet moment on the roads. In taxis I feared for the motorcyclists - we witnessed one of the 20,000 accidents motorcycles have there every year - and walking, even on pavements, I feared for my family. They even DRIVE MOTORCYCLES on the pavements, though this is illegal. And don't get me started about non-following of traffic lights. However.) History happened here; there are great views; it is beautifully laid-out, if almost mummified luxury. The Rough Guide brilliantly describes it as the lair of a James Bond baddie, but, as I mention, there was history here and really enjoyed this. Before enduring more of the bad air and appalling traffic, that is. But my advice, as elsewhere here, is to choose Hoi An instead. Wonderful place, Hoi An!!!!!

Nice historical experience Nice experience to learn more about government of Vietnam. The building is really nice, cost of entry is super cheap (40,000 VND) and there's lots of information cards around the place. Go down to the bunkers and up to the roof to see a nice aerial view. Good experience, you should go if you have many days in HCM. But if you're going for a quick trip there are better places (War Remnants Museum, Cu Chi Tunnels, Ben Thanh market, pagodas).

My wife and I took a self guided tour through the Independence Palace and enjoyed looking back at previous era. The Palace is well maintained, clean and easy to venture through. It may have been worth taking a guided tour, however it was easy to follow with the information provided at the various rooms. We added the Palace tour onto a day of adventuring HMC and saw lots that day. The tour cost about 30k Dong, ($2 NZD) and was a good way to spend a couple of hours looking back in time.

Walked Saigon today and decided to visit the palace. Entry fee is only about$2.00 plus we took the guided tour on a trolley for an extra $1.00. We the went inside the building . Good way to spent about an hour or so. Great place to take kids. They have a wonderful play ground and lots of grass to run on.

We visited here as it’s quite a symbolic place in Vietnam’s history but it’s not a very exciting g walk around/tour. They could do a lot more to make it interesting. Wouldn’t come back be not sure I would have done it if I had the chance again. The best thing is the tank on display that broke through the gates and unified North and South Vietnam

Intriguing Views for Americans Who Remember the War Many of the Americans now traveling in retirement remember news reports from Saigon, so a visit to the Independence Palace in Ho Chi Minh City is intriguing. Many of the rooms, like the grand piano at the roof-top dance pavilion and the helicopter pad used by the President of South Vietnam to review the military bases remind visitors of the time when American troops supported the South Vietnam government in a was against Communist North Vietnam. Other rooms bring the horrors of the massive bombing campaigns into sharp focus. Still other rooms speak to the diplomatic efforts that could not stop the war. The whole building, now, reflects the current government – which to an American visitor appears to be a tangled mix of strong government control with enormous entrepreneurial energy creating more and more goods and services for locals and visitors alike, including encouragement for tourists from both America and Asia.

Not very interesting.. if you are on a tight schedule I wouldn’t bother The Palace was not a “palace” it was just a large house frozen in time. If you have a week in HCMC than you should wander through. Otherwise I wouldn’t bother. You walk through the house and can peak in to a few rooms. The rooms state how they were used - by the President, VP, etc and some are cool to see but nothing special. We did NOT do the audio tour so maybe that contains a lot more history. Otherwise we just had a walkabout a house stuck in the 70s.
Can spend 45 minutes to 1 hour. Nothing very great . Just rooms and furniture. Snacks ) drink shop at top

Amidst a nice garden the palace: rebuilt in the 1960's it offers a view of luxury and diplomacy. Pictures went around the world when on 30 april 1975 a tank crached through the iron gates. Basement: the telecom centre and the war room. Ground floor: meeting rooms, upstairs one can see the many reception rooms. Second floor: card-playing room..., perished times.

Average looking structure The grounds of the palace are quite attractive but the main building itself is really nothing special. You have to take careful note of the opening hours to avoid the problem I faced of arriving when it was closed.

Average looking building but nice grounds The Independence Palace seems to close at lunchtime for a break, and this happened to be when I visited - so I wasn't able to go inside. But still entirely possible to take pics from the outside. The grounds are well cared for.

A lot of it was closed off, and there were only a few rooms you could see- it didn't really go into detail about the importance of the place, so do a little research before you go otherwise you are just confused
There may be historical significance to this site, but the palace itself is nothing great ... neither the architecture nor the opulence offers anything spectacular

This is the palace that the communists took over that ended the war in 1975. It's worth a visit, but there is not much to see since it is really just a large empty house with a lot of meeting rooms. It is interesting for the architecture, which was re-designed in the early 60's. I did not visit the underground bunker, because I did not read enough reviews ahead of time. I combined this with a trip to the War Remnants Museum and it is in walking distance.

OK but not first choice of attraction in Saigon It’s not a pretty building, there are some signs up next to rooms with a bit of historical info. It was a very humid day & there wasn’t much ventilation.
Ok but wouldn't make a special trip Like so many places associated with the pre communist era and the war there is very little information and what is there is skewed propaganda which is annoying
Well it was ok but not the best what we have seen in Vietnam. The entry is not too expensive and it is interesting, but if you missed it, never mind.

Palace itself is beautiful. Definitely pay the extra for the audio tour if you go. We did not get the audio tour, and I don't feel like we were really able to understand what we were looking and or learn much without it.

Compare this to the War Remnants Museum I went to the Palace and the War Remnants Museum in the same afternoon. Interesting contrast to see how the South Vietnamese leaders, diplomats and US personnel lived. Part of the palace is luxurious and ceremonial for diplomatic meetings. The basement sections are eerie. The communications area includes a "Message Correction" office. Of course both sides carried on lies and propaganda. It's an interesting contrast to living in tunnels and improvising forges to make weapons.

Average exhibition building Located within walking distance from Ben Thanh area, best time to visit is in the morning where there are still not crowded. There are few level showing different function of the section used in the palace. While in the palace ground, several tanks and aircraft which involved during liberation of South Vietnam also displayed. Entrance fee for adult VND40,000 while kid are charged for VND10,000.

Having just visited the Hi Chi Minh museum unguided and not really understanding what we saw, we decided to pay the extra fee for the audio guided tour of the Independence Palace. I wouldn't spend the money again, (even though it was only a small amount) because each room has the main parts of the commentary printed on a plaque to read for free. After the history and sometimes confronting nature of some of Ho Chi Minh's other tourist attractions this felt very underwhelming. Quite soul-less and conservative in comparison. Worth a quick look if you have the time but I wouldn't be upset if I missed it.
I expected this to be more of a museum tour but is is actually a Palace tour. Think white house on a smaller scale. Be sure to get the self guided tour with headphones.

We visited and whilst it was very interesting, we have been to better and more interesting places, such as the Museum in Havana. However, that being said, when in Rome etc. It is well worth a visit, to understand just how life was then and the complications involved.

Interesting but not vital to see The home of the South Vietnamese government allows visitors to see what things looked like at the time but there is precious little detail and some, obvious, favoring of the presentation of history. Not vital that you see it but if you have an hour to spare it is interesting.

This building’s association with the Vietnam war makes it one of the places you should visit. Interesting to see where all the key players would meet and the story how it was bombed towards the end of the war by a pilot from the north who had managed to infiltrate the South Viennese Air Force, then used one of their planes to attack the building.
Closed from 12 to 130. Didnt get to see anything because of this. We found a local tour guide who drove us to a near by pagoda. Good places for fushion food across the street

This was part of our guided tour and without him would not been able to do the place justice. It’s modern history and architecture but within most people’s living memory so it’s quite interesting Wouldn’t go out of my way to go there but as part of a schedule it passed some time

A showpiece of 20th Century Vietnamese history The building itself isn't extraordinary or compelling but the events that took place here and the backstories that go with them are worth your time if you want to understand more about present day Vietnam. Walking through the underground bunker, full of 1960s communications equipment, was what I enjoyed the most.
The palace which at the heart of times of resistance, was head of parliament. Very historical and interesting to see.
this is the place where you would know the life of their President in Ho chi Minh before they transfer t to hanoi such as meeting room, bedroom, living room, garden etc.,

A little underwhelming Wasn’t sure what to expect but had just left the war museum on the way home decided to drop in..... The bunker is a must see and you can feel the history that would of played out here and how it would of been used during the war.... The rest of the palace... wasn’t that great

Original palace would've been better! Built to replace the original French palace which was destroyed this is a modern 60s/70s style huge rambling building which, for me, is a bit soulless and characterless. It's interesting to hear a bit more history and see the tanks that stormed the gates (as seen on tv). Some insights to the formal aspects if government in what is the Vietnam equivalent of the White House. Not architecturally interesting to me, not particularly photogenic, but of general interest.
This palace showed how it looked back in the 70's down to the lacquered mural on the wall. There is even a bunker down below the palace that looks the same.

It is a cool place to see if you know the history and are interested in the last years of the republic of south vietnam. The bunker is cool, the historic meaning very interesting but the building itself is so so. If you have the time in hcmc and are interested in the history and already know something about it, you should go. Otherwise it can be skipped is my opinion. We did an audio tour, made it better but is a bit boring at some stops.

It is very attractive from the outside. Admission is cheap (couple bucks in usd). Not a whole lot to see really but the grounds and location are kept very well. There's a pretty comfortable park outside also kept pretty clean. Traffic in the area is pretty bad but that's the whole city I guess

Our tour was interesting. At least to get the correct facts around the war and all the other countries .... involvement in the Vietnam war. Place is well preserved and looked after. Interesting to see where and how things were planned and executed during that very rough and tough time in Vietnams history.

Visited the "Independence Palace" a few days ago. Entrance price was a 40.000 VND. The park around the palace is pretty nice. A few Vietnamese Army Tanks and a jet fighter. Nice to take some photos. Inside were a lot of people. what I expected. You can walk around freely in the building and see all the rooms. A bit boring ... They are just rooms with furniture inside them. Not really so interesting. What I did like was to see the bunker under the Palace and the communication rooms. Worth a visit .... but dont expect to much of it.
The palace is good to see, very large and full of memorabilia. It needs a guide to make the most of the facilities. Make sure you visit the bunker in the basement.
It was ok but not very interesting I is not take the audio guide bc it was really expensive for a museum

The palace is well maintained and there are guided tours available. You can also get audio guides for hire but they’re pretty expensive at 80,000 VND. It’s quite a big building and whilst a lot of the rooms are open there isn’t that much (interesting) to see. The bunker at the bottom has rooms with typewriters and maps but unfortunately I found it quite boring even though i actually like history. At least this place is in the centre of the city.

Interesting if you have time we enjoyed our visit but it's not the first thing I would recommend when visiting Ho Chi Minh City. If you have time, it's worth a walk through, won't take more than an hour
Does not seem like a Palace Its merely a building where president of earlier south Vietnam used to live and work during the Vietnam war . Seriously just waste of time.

We bought tickets around 11am today. We were told to skip the 1st floor entirely as that floor was closing for lunch. What we weren't told, was that the whole place was closing "for lunch". Absolutely ridiculous. The tour follows a loop around the palace... we had security guards usher us out of rooms shouting at us. At one point, we were walking down a corridor and the security guard just switched off all the lights and plunged us into darkness- I had to turn my phone torch on to see the way. If the management team are reading this - WHY do you sell tickets to tourists when you are going to push them out the door minutes later? I was really enjoying learning about the history there, but our visit was cut short by this ridiculous lunch time closing procedure. Be warned - if you visit - check how long it's open for.

Lots to see but audio guide is not worth the additional cost The palace is okay to visit if you have a few hours spare. We normally enjoy getting audio tours at these sites as usually they provide lots of extra snippets of information. However, this chap just read out the information for us providing no additional benefit! Additionally, the place is a maze! There are not enough signs to tell you the direction to go next so you end up missing things and then having to come back!

Seems more like parliament house as opposed to a palace This place doesnt have the feel of a palace especially if you are comparing to palaces in other countries. The rooms that are open are nice to look at with great signage up so you get some information. It is hot walking around as the palace is open not walled in as such.

The bunker experience The place is worthy of visiting specially to understand the strong communication system that existed at those times and how people protected themselves in the bunker during war times. The jeep and the Bentley were a treat to watch and how they were used by the president during the war time. The meeting room, bed room etc are very encouraging to see.

Its a nice building but there better sights to see in Ho Chi Minh City Its in a time warp. It looks like its been there for decades. Beautifully furnished as one would expect for a presidential palace - large reception rooms, beautiful furniture and carpets. Remember this is the place where the north Vietnamese army broke down the gates with their tanks to end the war. None of that is refelected as well as elsewhere. If you are stuck for time and want to use your time better understanding Vietnam and the war go to the War Remnants Museum amd spend the extra time there
En ok-attraction. Men kan ha det osett fast intressant when you inside. Had the best smoothies just outside.
The independence palace is worth a visit. Beautiful room decors to see and all the technology available then
This was another vietnam war themed place.It was well mantained and the grounds are beautiful.The underground bunker was well worth a look at

Faclities need to be updated About the title, I do understand the palace is meant to reflect the home and workplace of the President of South Vietnam during the Vietnam War. But the facilities for the now major tourist attraction is seriously lacking. I cant recall if there is 3 or 4 stories but the elevator was not working. Travelling with small kids, i had a sleeping toddler and had to carry the pram up single file flights of stairs! It was HOT! and i am from the Territory which has a tropical climate with high humidity and two seasons. IT WAS HOT! no aircon, no fans, no air movement. Location is good, walking distance . Grounds are beautiful. And palace is quite spectacular. Lots of important decisions have been made within those walls.
It is worth the visit if you have time. Basically a tour of rooms and their functions. Nice grounds around the palace.

Also called Reunification Palace, Presedential Palace, and we heard it also referred to as Liberation Palace...so trying to tell taxi driver where we wanted to go was difficult, we showed him on the map and then he understood. Interesting architecture, and a sad history, especially if you've seen the footage of the fall of Saigon. We have been told by many people it was actually a Russian tank that crashed the gates.

Well preserved presidential palace Interesting time capsule building as everything is as it was in April 1975 when the Vietnam War ended. Highly recommend getting the audio guide to wear while walking around. It's a very popular so busy attraction.
Kinda interesting... pretty boring... not really much to see. We got bored quite quickly and left to go see/do something cool. Don't be sad if you miss it.

The Independence Palace was worth a look,the grounds are beautiful, and its easy to get around with several vantage points for each room. Its a shame there is no good spot to get photos of the helicopter on the roof

This is a lovely building seeped in history. It gives you an insight into the politics of the era. Take the time to read the descriptions outside the rooms. There are stairs to climb but lifts are available for the less able bodied.

Historical perspective The Palace is really more interesting from a historical perspective than architectural (unless you like 1950s brutalist buildings). But from the tanks on the lawn (the ones that smashed through the gates in 1975) to the underground bunker, it does bring some understanding of Vietnamese history.
This wasn't really us but we went anyway the interesting part was the basement with all the secret communication area . Very grand for it's time

Nothing much here, place is still well-taken care of but there's nothing much that caught my interest. Take a few photos here and there, listen to audio tour while you're visiting each room, that's probably just it.
Obviously an important historical site and good to see it has remained pretty much as it is since then. This is bit a royal palace, but all the same it has historical meaning behind itself

Wow, quite amazing rebuild though in looking at the outside not a traditional palace you would normally think of as not full of gold and those typical al palace features. Yes great architecture though more like a government building. Inside the rooms are amazing which clearly differentiates to how it looks on the outside, worth a visit but you won't be blown away

This is the old president palace- when south Vietnam had one. The palace was rebuilt some time back in the 1900 century and built in a modern architecture. You find some stories from the past but again all is filtered by the government thought and ideas. On the top you find a helicopter and each floor have a unique story- so bring your guide with you. Entering cost a small fee paid at the gate

More of a Gov't building than a Palace It is an interesting building, lots of walking and stairs (lifts are small and sparse). Most of the furniture and things were lost post war and much of what is there was put in there for the viewing. You will not find this opulent etc, not that kind of palace. Make sure you get into the basement this was most interesting to us...more war related things.
A place to see if you need to pass time I wouldn't say it is a must see, but if you are looking for places to visit and have time to pass, i would recommend going here.

Interesting to see inside the rooms of the palace Good to see inside the former presidents quarters; particulary the bunker. Our guide also showed us the entrance to the underground escape tunnels which are said to go for miles but this is not official (access not allowed). I recommend having a guide to explain the significance of each room/area.
A building with lots of tables and chairs which was in the 1960's. It's okay to see but not much going on.

If you are into history it's a good visit. The only problem being it has little information on what went on here with the most informative thing being a documentary you can watch. Just prepare yourself and enjoy the historic site

Interesting place to visit , if you have the time The Palace is interesting mostly from an historical point of view. You can learn here about the regime changes in Vietnam and modern days wars. The Palace design is nice but not too impressive. The military bunker underground is intriguing. It's also nice to see lots of 60 years old electronics and communication equipment.

Not a great deal to see A little underwhelmed by the Palace. It's quite plain, pleasant but not much to inspire or excite. We didn't have a tour guide so maybe we missed out on the story, but we did read our travel guides in detail. Couldn't find the telecoms section which was apparently on a lower floor (as guide book said) and gave up as it was stiflingly hot even with areas of air con. You only need an hour to see the main rooms.

Interesting historical place. The more you know about the history of this place the more interesting a visit would be. It covers government offices, accommodation and military command areas. A bit of ground to cover and it has been maintained as it would have been in the early 1970's.

Although this is an impressive building I did find it a bit dour. There are a couple of tanks in the grounds, but aside from that it's just a few rooms which you can look in from their entrances. Not sure what I expected, but the reality was a pretty boring experience. Ok to be able to cross off my list, and the movie room gave a quick burst of air con, but I wouldn't say this is a must do activity. You can get adequate pictures of it from the road as a tourist....

So where the war was won- with the tanks coming through the main gates after an earlier bombing. You can see the main cabinet room etc but rooms/floors closed when I visited so found it of limited value

It's quite a large complex but rather boring. The basement bunker seems to be just props not actually how it used to be. You can't walk out to the helicopter landing pad either. Some interesting history but it just felt boring and stale.

Important Piece of History The Palace was an important piece of history for the end of the Vietnam war. If you have a guide you get some of the history about the tank crashing through the gate and the reason for the airplane onsite. Without a guide, it's just another govt. building with conference rooms and offices... The bunker/bomb shelter is interesting. I would think 30 minutes is plenty here...

We find it ok to visit, there explanations about the different state rooms. I found the most interesting area, the Bunker as shows all the different communication tools. Price: 200,000 (200.000 VND)overpriced in my opinion. Worth a visit if you have the time and are interested in the history of things and what this symbolised.

You need to see this palace to understand more what transpired during the Vietnam War. It has a big part played in what Vietnam is today. A must see is the bomb proof bunker, where the South President was secured during the war. You will see old communications left by the Americans. Also very near the Central Post Office and Notre Dame Cathedral.

Personally i could have read the history from my PC Frankly speaking, it exhibited a meeting room, amongst others. Was not impressed with that. What was impressive to me is the garden which was quite matured and beautiful. They had a variety of trees, my favourite was the Frangipani tree. To those who like me are not fascinated by the chairs and tables, go and walk around the park/garden and sit in the benches and enjoy. Well worth it.

This is a lovely "renovated/rebuilt landmark near the center of Ho Chi Minh. Although an interesting place to visit, to see how the last ruler lived, entertained and in fact kept an eye on the troops during the last war it is not particularly suited to younger children. Teenagers however may take something to remember as did we. This is a modern looking building with an older "as it was" interior. It has well kept grounds. If you like recent history then this is a good visit but other wise just "OK"

The Independence Palace (also known as the Reunification Palace) was in a tour we took. It is an impressive building with lots of highly decorated rooms (60's styles), but I could have given it a miss.
A big palace. More understand history of Vietnam. This palace will be closed during lunch hour and reopen after that. Not so convenient for tourists.
Well not much info, can't really go into the rooms. Didn't really get much out of it. Go the war museum instead
A good place to visit if you have extra time though it is different from War Remnants Museum but I like War Remnants Museum more. Entrance fee cost 20,000 VND and opens at 1 in the afternoon.
Kind of interesting, but not great for kids. Not air conditioned so also hot, mostly just period furniture.

Was a nice landmark. Interior furniture shows the lavishness of the president. Very big, around 120,000 sq feet. I took the tour around with a small tourist car, very cheap and the driver was friendly. The set off was that it was too crowded.

So this is the Palace? Not a very impressive display of organisation or lavishness of where the last government of the south resided. Amusing was the bombing of the palace with a stolen aircraft. Irritating were the tourists who were always standing in front of me to take their picture or posed each time I wanted to a picture. As a technical communications person, I was not impressed by the equipment that was displayed.

Full of communist propaganda... Nice to see nice to visit but I don't think I'll ever be going back. If you read some of the exhibit descriptions is full of communist propaganda. This is Southern Vietnam but the museum will talk about how to communist defeated the Americans and the southern Vietnam. So full of crap
This was included in our city cyclo tour. It is yet another nice building and calm place in the middle of a hectic city.
Impressive building, living area of leader, there also have playground for kid in the garden. Water puppet show also hidden in the garden with economy price

One of the main monuments in Ho Chi Min City There is a small charge to get in; there are the main rooms, all originally funrnished & quite opulent which you are able to see. There are information plaques in English explaining each room. Good views from the top. Don't walk on the grass!

Nothing exciting here. It was cool to see what everything looked like 60 years ago, but to be honest, it was like walking through a time capsule. It was cool to say we went, but I have no reason to ever revisit. Not to mention it wasn't free.
This is a decent visit if you have some time to spare. War remnants museum told a more interesting story.

We went on a tour with a guide but I think you could do it just as well on you own. Though not terribly exciting it is kind of a place you just have to visit when you are there and is definitely a place frozen in time. We only had about 45mins there but 1-1/2hrs or so would get you through the lot and time to watch some of the documentaries down stairs. But you wouldn't want any longer than that!

This palace isn't as grand as you would expect from the word palace and certainly does not invoke the historical richness of other palaces around the world. it is inexpensive visiting though and if you happen to be staying nearby, it is worthwhile taking a look.
Can't say I was won over with this one it's worth a visit but theres only so many rooms one can look at I think this building is to modern to be of any historical interest.

It's ok to visit I found it very hot inside and with not that much to see inside, some of the rooms are impressive and I'm glad I was able to get on to the roof. At the moment the bunker was closed due to maintenance (June 2017) so I cannot comment on that aspect of the building. More for history buffs than casual tourists.

Visiting the palace gives an insight into the past and how the president used to live before war took place. The building is well maintained and speaks volumes about war struggle faced by the country. It is one of the must visit places in ho chi minh city.
Persevered since the end of hostilities. This place a reminder of the war and 70's interior decorating. Inexpensive and interesting. Takes about an hour.

Museum in the 'raw'. No denying that it portrays the past and the situation back in history. Certainly does make one realize how fortunately we are to be during that time in Vietnam. Should visit while there. Doesn't cost a lot. Just check on their opening times. Was caught out before during their lunch break.
went a wander to the palace and I suppose it was nice to see, if you have time to spare its worth a look.
Photos from outside are enough Take your photos outside and walk by. Really not worth your dong and time going inside even if you are interested in this nations political history.
We do not went inside the building, from outside the Independence Palace looks nice and looks well maintained

Good place to spend a few hours Although not intending to visit the palace, with a few hours to kill we decided to pay the small enterence fee and have a look inside. The building itself is very pleasing , with many of the rooms a good insight into vietnamese politics (with a strong communist theme as one may expect). However, despite some rooms looking spectacular, the overall experience felt somewhat lacking. Worth a visit if you have the time though.

Nice Building To Tour But No Big Whup This is the former Presidential Palace of the defunct South Vietnamese Government. Building was designed by a local architect and replaced the old French-inspired building. As an example of modern Asian architecture it's perfectly pleasant. Most American visitors won't really relate to the building's history -- it's big claim to fame was when a defecting pilot attempted to bomb it and the breaching of the gates by Viet Cong tanks in 1975, which basically marked the end of the Vietnam War. So an okay visit but just not particularly significant to (at least) American visitors.
This is the place where the Vietnam war ended and as such it is a historic place. You will find meeting rooms, bunker, war rooms.
Another trip worth the effort based purely on history. Its amazing how 'important' people live when the rest of the country was dying from war and hunger
This Palace is a reminder of the war and the past. It's impressive but cold. The only emotion attached to this building is how badly the war ended.

Not really wow once you are there A very nice place to visit. Good to know that they close during lunchtime. The place is very old and a little rundown. Pretty much more like a wealthy place for a successful person decorated in the 60s style. The place do not offer air-conditioning and it was very hot when we were there so we quickly visited all rooms and left.

Wandering the Empty Halls An interesting place to visit and get a sense of days gone by. Be prepared to be very hot...no air-conditioning in this beautiful 60's building. Loved the architecture...very cleverly designed for the heat as well as lovely on the eye. The 7 Year old was a bit bored with the wandering, although did like the walk down into the bunker! Nice little sidewalk cafes on the outside of the grounds to slake the thirst from walking the Palace.

Vietnam's White House I love this big, wide, simple yet magnificent palace.It takes 20 to 30 minutes to tour all the rooms.I believe this building dose not have air conditioning and has no lifts,in this hot ,humid weather you'd better bring water with you at all times,some restrooms do not supply toilet paper so be prepared.
Building has interesting history. Well kept just as it was used many years ago. Entrance costs very little.
There are some nice rooms in the building, you can see quite a few of them. Check the opening times before you go, because they close around lunch time.

Fun to see old communication equipment used to end war We saw the beautiful old stylish Presidential Place which became the Independence Palace. What was most interesting to me was a seeing the old communication equipment used in negotiating an end to the war.

We enjoyed our visit here - especially some of the more unexpected rooms such as the meditation room at the top of the palace that got converted into a party room and the bomb shelter in the basement. We were also surprised by the interior style which was interesting to learn about. Not a huge amount of info though so perhaps better with a guide. Large building so despite tourists still relatively peaceful to walk around. Cheap enough entry fee and some decent views of leafy areas of HCMC from the roof.

Interesting place, but a little sparse This was a cool place to walk around and the grounds are lovely. It's pretty interesting to see, but I didn't feel like I learnt all that much. The bunker is cool, but there's very little in there and not much explanation. Worth going if you have a few days here, but not really if you're short of time!
On most tourist itineraries, this is mildly interesting but hardly a must see location. One to do rapidly and spend more time at the War Remnants Museum.
This place is ok even if you see from outside. 40000 VND is too much ticket price for this.
Historic landmark but very busy and congested Pick your time well as we went during Liberation Day so it was very congested which spoiled things a bit for photography
Had a private guided tour including this building. Lots of furniture and some historic interest. Guide presented the "party line" in poor English.
We had a really good guide which helped view this non too impressive building. Do get to see behind the scenes and well documented on the walls which helps explain the history and significance.

This was the first stop on our tour with our private guide and was ok. To be honest there is not much to see other than a few rooms where the former president used to greet diplomats and live. Was expecting something more grand than we saw! War rooms are the most interesting part in the basement. Practical looking building but interesting to understand the historical significance of it from our guide.
