Palace that is a key symbol of the North beating the South in the Vietnam War. Not the prettiest palace in the world. Interesting especialy the bunker system.
This building was built in the 1960s so it was architecturally boring. I prefer buildings that are older and architecturally interesting. This building was of a type I could see back home.
Went to the independence Palace and basically it shows all the kind of rooms with very simple furnitures. Personally I didn't really enjoy or learnt any history about Vietnam.

We toured around by ourselves and it was well-signed which helped us to understand the history of the palace. A lot of the rooms are 'same same'. Worth a visit but it only took us an hour to see the four flours, bunker room and grounds.

A Quick Tour Suffices Independence Palace also known as Reunification Palace is definitely a quick stop. Took us back to the sixties and the retro era of furnishing / interior and architecture. The landscape is kept neat and trimmed. Not as interesting as the other sites in the area. Would just allocate 45 mins to an hour for this place. There are many restaurants nearby this site which make for an ideal stop for afternoon drink or meal to escape the Saigon heat. Pop by Propaganda Bistro - located just 2 minutes walk from the Palace. Nice watering hole - plus other restaurants just across the park.

Nice rooms but bit boring after 10 mins It cost 40k dong each to get in. All rooms were nice but all very similar so got a bit boring. Worth noting close at 12-1 for lunch as many other attractions do. Spent 30 minutes in there and felt to long. Would recommend going to war museum next to it which is much better

Not sure why the tour guide dropped us off here The palace is a concrete functional sixties style building that isn't particularly remarkable except for several very long dining tables on display. Not air conditioned. No doubt, would have been more interesting with a guide who explained the significance of the exhibits. A lot of the building was empty.
A nicely maintained but poorly presented "museum " good views from the top floor ok hot/rainy day activity

It was intersting to see since we watched the fall of Saigon. The Palace has some interesting plaques talking about the war and many visitors. Of course, the most memorable part was watching people trying to board the Huey near the roof. Now there is a Huey on the roof along with Soviet tanks near the gate. Very reminiscent.
Like watching paint dry Had a walk around, looked at a few poorly decorated rooms, saw where the ex president his during the invasion. Certainly don't need to go again... Try it, you might like it!

Honestly it was a waist of time , the building has nothing interesting to offer and the decoration inside was all 70 s and it was nothing amazing. Very blank furniture , i guess most of them were just put there to fill in the rooms. Not worth going and visiting it .

A beautiful building in lovely grounds. A look inside the rooms of keaders and the relevant persons within the era of the Vietnam war. Rooms are nice including reception lounges for official meetings but you just meander past them. The bunker toom was the most intetesting ad you could imagine this as a hive of decisions and communications during the war. Visit if you have time.

Historically accurate Wow, go down to the bottom level- the best place. They've kept all the war stuff from the USA down there, as well as Vietnam memorabilia. The upstairs rooms are quite boring and nothing special. But the interesting part is in the basement! Our guide was fab in here!!!
Was taken on a conducted tour....must not do this unless you have a lot of time & energy....lost interest as it was too long & boring. Very well maintained & full of tourists all the time

Only if you've got lots of time Maybe I am just cranky because it was incredibly hot and humid yesterday, when I visited. I have some mobility issues and the elevator was out of order. Interesting period Furnishings, and certainly

We didn't realise this was closed 11am-1pm and it caught us out one day so beware. This was actually quite expensive compared to some of the other tourist sites in Ho Chi Minh. It was interesting to see and I thought the bunker was the best bit but it could have done with more information around the building. Also I felt the building lacked character, it was not very exciting to look around. Ok to see, but not a highlight.

Good to see, but nothing crazy going on here. As the title suggests.... It was informative, but nothing life changing. If you have time, check it out. It costs 40000VND for adults, and take about 2hrs to walk through the tour.

Independence Palace Good for a Tame Experience This Palace seemed to be on the well worn tourist trail with numerous Tour groups and buses of visitors. Not really our cup of Tea but very well maintained and clean as. In a Country where many folk seem to struggle to feed their Family it is easy to be dismayed as the apparent opulence.

Not set up for a self guided tour. Sometimes it was difficult knowing which to go and many times the explanations were quite brief. However, I found the kitchen very interesting with very large burners ,huge woks and mixers to prepare food for banquets and for the Presidential family. The grounds are well manicured with grass, topiary and flowers. Even a helicopter is on the roof of one part of a lower building and on the grounds is an airplane and tank.

Located near Benh Tahn Market, a 15 minutes walk. This place is a tourist hotspot. A reasonable walk from Museums and other attraction. Nothing especially unique.. but a visit is worthy still if not for the historical value..
I could have missed this Massive area, gardens beautiful but frankly very ordinary. If time poor, miss this tour out. Very clean but sterile and one felt that no one ever lived there.
Nothing much. Perhaps good for architectural enthusiast. The building is of colonial design and a huge lawn.
This was good to see and inexpensive. Lots of rooms and floors to visit, allow yourself about an hour to an hour and a half.

This was rather underwhelming. It just has lots of meeting rooms and then living quarters on the upper levels, never really felt any connection to the historical events that happened here. You're not missing out on much if you don't visit this. Next time I'll pass.
Just another museum. Found this to be quite boring and full of other tourists. Would be best to go on a weekday to avoid some of the crowds

The old palace is sited on what was a beautiful French inspired palace but got knocked down and replaced by a top Vietnamese architect in the 1960's - and unfortunately it's not very pleasing to the eye. You can wander through the whole palace - 4 floors (including a helicopter on the roof and a bunker in the basement) - you get to see the President's receiving rooms, living quarters, personal cinema and shooting gallery etc. The basement bunker I thought the most intriguing seeing the old war maps and communication systems

Is ok as its one of the things everybody goes to when they visit hcmc, I that some of the rooms were great and gave an invite what it was like, again I felt that many of the things were in dis repare, with just a little work I am sure they would be much better, the ppl around the area dont seem to interested

Always a bit strange when ‘history’ is presented when it all happened in your own lifetime , all within a building which was a mirror of my local Technical college in early 1970s’ . Would not recommend unless some real interest in the period ..
So what's the big deal? Nice grounds, nice large rooms with chairs all arranged in a rectangular pattern but leaves you with a "So what" feeling.

old south presidential palace is now reunification palace Our tour was just a little interesting but really it's just full of 1970s furniture. I found the controll rooms of interest as they had very antiquated electrical and communication equipment on show. This is evidence of how far we have come since 1970. The guides push the government line of the glorious victory over the enemy but it gets a bit tired after a while.

The building didn't do much for us, however go for a walk around the grounds. Massive trees, birds, some military equipment. There is a coffee shop to the left before you walk up the stairs to the entrance of the building.This coffee shop also has a gift stall and musicians playing. The road outside is a great photo opportunity with the tre lined road and beautifully decorated lights.

Best thing was the bunker This palace was built in the 60s and in my humble opinion, there haven't been too many buildings built in that era that have been pleasing to look at. I appreciate that they've kept everything from that time for us to see what it was like then. I think the bunker was definitely worth visiting for the role it played in the war. It's quite stuffy inside despite having the windows open.

Entry cost was 40,000 for us and worth it for being able to spend a good hour looking inside. From ground floor up you get opulent rooms and a balcony with a view, and below ground level are the bunkers the president used during the bombings still with old fashioned decor.
40,000 dong entry fees. Huge palace, clean and nice. Mostly shut in the afternoon at around 12:30-2:00.
Historical significance but little content Worth seeing and spending about an hour here. Lots of English signage, but little content in the way of the history of the building, etc.
Not much to see. Dining rooms. Board room type rooms. Hot and humid. No air conditioning. We were on a guided tour and we skipped out halfway through to get some fresh air.

the ancient presidential palace, Saigon Vietnam If you are staying in Saigon, chances ares this spot is already on your list of must see places. Most of the travelers who come to Vietnam are interested in its history, and this building is a symbol of it : whatever your age, you have seen the photo representing the end of the vietnam war - the longest and most well known of the 20th century - a north vietnamese tank crushing the main gate of this former presidential palace. Everyday busloads of tourists flood the place from early morning, they are then distributed into groups by language, because they will follow a guide who will lead you through the palace, explaining everything to you like a guide should do. You will see many different halls, and in the underground you will see a military operation room dating back to the 60s with all the equipment of this time. You will also go to the terrace, where you can see the former president's personal chopper, naturally a Bell Huey. After that, you could tell your friends you have visited that historical place.
Historic building but may not be appealing to kids. It was very hot especially inn the basement "offices".

The fall of Saigon is a special moment in history. At the centre of the fall are the activities at and from the Presidential Palace, now call the Independence Palace. A must see and must feel attraction
we visited to palace as part of an organised tour. It was the end of a long hot day and we found it slightly underwhelming. That said some of the interiors were beautiful.

You kind of have to see it If you are visiting the city you feel like you have to make time to see this place. I struggled between rating 3 and 4. The history deserves a 4 but the place itself, only a 3 as it's a bit boring. The views from the top are nice and the basement bunker areas are a bit interesting.

If your old enough to remember the 'last helicopter leaving Saigon pictures' on the news or the red flag being hoisted on the balcony then you might like to see those spots for real. It's an easy walk around in a large government building. There is nothing too exciting to see but you are walking around a building which was central to the Vietnam war and it's an easy walk in the centre of town with nice grounds

Did not do a lot for me. There are a number of interesting rooms with historical connections, but you need a guided tour to make sense of it. The bunker is intersting and there are good views from the top

Basically you are allowed to see the interior of the old Presidential Palace used by the President of North Vietnam. This place is significant as the Vietnam War ended here when a South Vietnam tank burst into its gates. The palace itself is a display of rooms with furnished as they were. You don't need too much explanation. The bunkers also can be visited and you can see communication equipment and telex from that time.

It cost 40,000 VND to enter. Be mindful that it will close for an hour over lunch so factor that into your plans (we didn't realise, and had to leave as we were watching the documentary!). It's ok - again not an amazing attraction. If you have seen other palaces around the world you may be disappointed by the lack of extravagance. There are a few rooms set up - sparsely decorated considering who the palace was for. It's quite hot as there's not a lot of air conditioning. The grounds are pretty, and it's a nice place to sit and relax under the trees away from the hustle and bustle of HCMC traffic.
This was a really interesting visit. We didn't do a tour but everything is pretty self explanatory. Definitely a must see while in Vietnam.

A Symbol of Corruption This grandiose building is a testament to the sordid past of the South Vietnamese higherarchy during the 60's and 70's with its grand meeting rooms and even a US helicopter on its roof. Many staairs to climb since the private elevator from the President's office to the bunker in the basement is no longer in use. Great view of the city from the rooftop "lounge" which at one time was used as a dance floor - complete with a space for the dance band.
Its a very good preserved historical palace which shows how president lived during independence time.Although it is not that large and decorative it is worth seeing.
I don't think I would have gone to see this site if it wasn't included in the tour. If you like 60's decor you may enjoy. I couldn't help but think:" who paid for this building"?
Just a big 4-Floor building full of big conference rooms you can not enter..just view the room by the door.
If you're short on time, I wouldn't recommend this place. There's not a whole lot of information, or much to see overall.

Note that you need to move in order: 1st floor, 2nd floor etc. We headed to the basement took elevator from bunker and kitchen level till 4th floor and guards did not let us go to the 3rd floor anymore from there. However, from the roof there is a nice view to the city.

Most of the monuments in Saigon are in the same area in the center of town and you can walk to all of them . The independence palace is one of them. There is a small entry fee and one gets to see a bunch of halls with 70s furniture. The basement is the bunker where you find out dated radio and electronic equipment. Not a must see, but there is only that much sight seeing one can do in Saigon, so go and take a look. 30 Minutes max.

It's an interesting place especially that it played a huge role in Vietnamese history. You'll find spacious meeting rooms, conference rooms, offices. They even have a game room and their own theater! You'll also get the chance the see the president's room and the HUGE walk-in closet! The palace is well-maintained and kept very clean. For some reason, the elevators were not working when we went there. Also, the place could use additional (industrial) fans to keep the place more ventilated. Entrance fee applies. I would suggest you go here on your own as it is fairly easy to explore the place. Not necessary to join a tour. 😁
The rooms in this building are amazing as well as the grounds. There is also some aircraft in the grounds.

History of architecture, interior design and planning Great building with a good surrounding garden. Those who like buildings with great surrounding should go to this place. And ofcourse it was one of important places in Ho Chi Minh City and Vietnam.

It is not the original Palace, it is basically a new Palace that replace the French Colonial Style. You can visit all four floors and the rooftop and well a the Bunker. Each floor has well appointed state rooms. Is it a must see, the inside not really.
Nice to walk by. Didn't bother to pay just to walk the grounds. Seemed ridiculous when I could walk by On the outside and see, guided tour inside would be a nice offering
If you would like to learn more about the history of Vietnam and see their meeting rooms go and check it out.

Lots of walking and stair climbing. Go only if you are interested in history of Vietnam war. Not a fancy place compared to other palaces. Main attraction is what happened here before, during and after Vietnam war. If you are not interested in that war, then you will get bored quickly. There is a long walk from the park entrance to the building, and inside the buildings. Lots of climbing multiple stories up and down narrow stairwells, and sometimes having to wait for another tour group to pass. Would not have gone if had know this beforehand.

This is a very modern building with history of the Vietnam war. All the rooms are modern, large with furniture. When you enter the ground floor, there are 2/3 rooms to view. The directions are poor and we initially thought that was it You have to walk to one end where you will find stairs to the second and top floor, these are easily missed so be careful not to miss these All the rooms are very much the same, it's all a bit boring to be honest and a bit disappointing. The top floor has a helicopter and the grounds have a couple of tanks That's it I'm afraid to say. Fortunately the entrance fee is only 40,000 dong, about 1 GBP so it's not a budget breaker. Its close to the War museum so you can do both in the day, note that both shut for lunch so check the times on the website
Huge infrastructure in Vietnam where the rooms are definitely a must see. However you would appreciate it more if you walk with a tour guide.
Check out the underground bunkers! Apart from that a little boring and not worth your time because there aren't much information unless you have a guide, but it's good for its history!

The Former President's Palace Neither the inside nor the outside could be said to be architectural masterpieces. The site used to be the site of the colonial governor's mansion. Was then rebuilt as the President's (of South Vietnam) palace. at the end of the Vietnam war, the tanks from the north crashed the gates and the building became a museum. The tanks parked outside are not real but look the part. The grounds are beautifully maintained and parkland runs on beyond the palace walls. Nice setting. Inside you walk from one stateroom full of grand furniture to another. After a couple they seem to just blur into one. Of more interest are the upper floors and the bunker in the basement. The upper floors house the president's personal rooms and give a slightly more personal feel. Including his personal escape helicopter. In the basement there are rooms where the president and his staff ostensibly ran the war with the north. More probably via the many phones to the US military.

Although there is some mildly interesting history to this building, we were at a loss at to why so many people were taking multiple photos of what were, essentially, soulless furniture showrooms. Architecturally of the 1950's, it has no real wow factor, and having been dropped there for an hour by our tour guide, we spent 40 minutes of that waiting to be picked up again.

An average building with historical importance It is the place where the sentence of reunification was stated so the building holds a great significance . It has many halls where the president used for various purposes. He used to stay there so the rooms are arranged accordingly. It is quite interesting to see how the bunker was used in emergency.

From outside it looks like a 60's building a view confirmed when you get inside. The building has been faithfully returned to it's original form having been bombed in 1975. You get a real sense of what daily life was like for the leader and his family. Lot's of pieces from the time, tanks, cars, even a helicopter. There not the actual vehicles mostly but are the same models.

another palace can be like another church and this one we all agreed was less than worthwhile ... nothing impressive or different ... another 'something to do" experience ... we would have rather walked through the city

Where are the package tourists? HERE are the package tourists... The Independence or Reunification Palace is a very important building historically but inside you'll find very little information about it (like 2 paragraphs from Wikipedia). What you will find there is - nice gardens with 2 tanks and a plane - interior design from the 1960s of a presidential palace - LOTS OF CLUELESS PEOPLE... This place is on the top of the things-to-do list in Saigon because its one of the few places where you can stop with a tour bus and 40 pax. If you're a package / big bus tourist you'll visit the palace anyways. If you are an independent traveler you might give it a look (I didn't regret it) but don't add an extra day to your itinerary just to see this palace and couple other rather average sights.

It's the palace of the president with all the furniture and devices from the time of the Vietnamese war which were used during it. It's interesting how they could live at those critical years and see these things from the seventies. You can walk from the basement till the roof and have a nice view to the garden from the top.
A little too long and boring The history was interesting but the tour was boring. Rooms of furniture, nothing more. Take it or leave it.

A beautiful slick building with a history that is more impressive than the edifice itself. It was the presidential residence before two North Vietnam tanks (that are displayed out front) knocked down the iron gates in 1975. The inside of the building itself is worth a quick look for its award winning design and materials used. It is build in 1962 on the site of the former palace that was bombed by two traitorous South Vietnam fighter pilots in early 1962.

Plenty of floors and rooms or halls with authentic or semi-authentic historical decor. As usual, limited access to the rooms at the doorway. Plenty of info in english. A bit shabby and contrived wartime control center display in the rooms in the basement. A couple of tanks in the front yard. A good museum but not-so-overwhelming main attraction in the city. Worth the visit though. Small kids won't probably appreciate.
It's okay, not great, but if your a history lover In would recommend it. Brings some perspective in regards to the southern Vietnamese perspective on the Vietnam war

The Presidential Palace An interesting tour with plenty of history from the Vietnam war. Many elegant rooms with excellent pieces from history. The bunker with its communication center is especially interesting. A must see for history buffs.

I was a little disappointed after visiting the Independence Palace. When visiting, you are essentially touring the hallways of the palace and get to peak inside the presidential rooms from the hallway. There was nothing extraordinary about the rooms or the palace itself. I would definitely choose the War Museum over the visit to the palace.

Not the best use of your spare hour I am not a big architectural buff and my review might be a bit biased. I found the history behind the Palace pretty interesting, but didn't find the building half as much. there are like hundreds of giant rooms spread over 4 levels which you can see from outside as you are not permitted to enter. It gets a bit monotonous and lackluster after a couple of rooms. But the basement is a must visit place in the palace as it houses the Saigon's president's Merc car and the bunker. There are some photo opportunities from the balcony and in front of the building.
if you are looking for vietnam especially ho chi minh political history maybe you can spend your time here and the ticket is about 40.000 dong

The independence palace showcases the political history of Vietnam and provides a brief peek at the country's cultural history. Many local tourism agencies include this attraction in their itinerary, so it can get quite crowded at times. Even though it is called a "palace", I think the word "mansion" is much more suitable. The rooms can get pretty repetitive after awhile, so I would recommend allocating around 30-45 min to tour the entire place. Taking a look at the underground tunnel is a must if you intend to visit.
Lovely, but crowded and hot! This is a lovely palace with an interesting history. The building is well organized, but crowded with tourists and probably not good for a hot day!

Worth an hour if you're in the area An homage to 1960's architecture, although as it's not in use other than a tourist attraction it's soulless. Lots of mildly impressive reception rooms, akin to a slightly fuddy old hotel that needs updating. The underground bunker is the most interesting, full of 1960's communications equipment from the US. The grounds are pleasant to walk in.

Allow yourself plenty of time to walk around the palace and gardens. The meeting rooms at the palace are still used so not all rooms were available on the day we visited to view. The decoration in my view is very opulent and ahead of its time in many respects. The furniture is well maintained and all rooms have clear narratives written in English and Vietnamese. Do not miss the trip to the bunker where you will see how the world has moved on in terms of communication. Worth a tour when visiting Ho Chi Ming.

Interesting but not compelling This large building was the pre-war equivalent of the White House and is now used only as a museum and event venue. Other the providing a chance to enter a time warp into the 1960s, there was not much of interest. It was generally free of agitprop but the commentary signage did not provide much historical information other than saying what the rooms were used for. An okay visit but not a must see.

Get know about the history of this place through the tour guide. The meaning behind the architectural design and also have a peek on the basement communication system they using previously during war time . Not suitable for those who do not wish to know the history of the palace and also HCMC.

Something I wouldn't do again It cost us 40000 dong to enter and was definitely a let down. It was a beautiful building but every single room was just a different set up of furniture, chairs and tables and an occasionally there would be a room with a slightly different theme. All in all though it was quite boring and I would not go again. I guess it is something to do just once, but I wouldn't recommend it.

This is probably one of those locations you need to do when in Saigon. It was interesting but not as good as other activities we have done. It's a nice palace but have seen better in other places around the world. Given its inexpensive to enter probably worth doing and you can finish in an hour. Impersonally preferred the war museum over this.

Not as interesting as we'd thought it would be Had a guide inside, but there are plenty of signs to help you through. I wasn't really interested in the living quarters or meeting rooms, and there are lots of those. The map and phone rooms, and bunkers are a little more interesting, but if you are trying to fit lots of things into your visit, this may not be one to add.
Near the church and post office drive is good but not worth going inside. Architecture isn't anything special in my opinion but a great piece of history.
